description: distractor batch 7 - fixed income: FI overview, LDI/index-based, credit strategies, yield curve strategies (109 items); parallel-weight distractors authored; correct key pinned to post-rotation layout
precondition: apply after batch_01, batch_02, rotate_drill_keys.py, batch_03, batch_04, batch_05, batch_06

options.A: the process of structuring and managing a fixed-income bond portfolio to minimize the variance in the realized rate of return over a known time horizon.
options.B: Immunization is the practice of maximizing the expected return of a bond portfolio without regard to the timing of any obligations.
options.C: Immunization refers to eliminating credit risk by holding only government-guaranteed securities.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the process of structuring and managing a fixed-income bond portfolio to minimize the variance in the realized rate of return over a known time horizon; this variance arises from the volatility of future interest rates; default risk is neglected because portfolio bonds are assumed to have default probabilities approaching zero.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Immunization is the process of structuring and managing a fixed-income bond portfolio to minimize the variance in the realized rate of return over a known time horizon. This variance arises from the volatility of future interest rates. Default risk is neglected at this point because the portfolio bonds are assumed to have default probabilities that approach zero.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Immunization targets a KNOWN horizon obligation and minimizes return variance to it — return maximization is not the objective.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The technique addresses INTEREST RATE risk over a known horizon, not credit risk elimination.

options.A: A parallel yield curve shift is a necessary condition for immunization to succeed — without it, immunization always fails.
options.B: a sufficient, but not necessary, condition for immunization is a parallel (or shape-preserving) shift whereby all yields change by the same amount in the same direction.
options.C: Yield curve shifts bear no relation to whether an immunized portfolio achieves its target return.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Parallel (shape-preserving) shifts are SUFFICIENT but not necessary — immunization can succeed under some non-parallel movements.
rationales.B:
Full statement: a sufficient, but not necessary, condition for immunization is a parallel (or shape-preserving) shift whereby all yields change by the same amount in the same direction; if change in cash flow yield is the same as that on zero-coupon bond being replicated, immunization can be achieved even with non-parallel shift to yield curve.

Correct. Per the reading Summary: 'A sufficient, but not necessary, condition for immunization is a parallel (or shape-preserving) shift whereby all yields change by the same amount in the same direction. If the change in the cash flow yield is the same as that on the zero-coupon bond being replicated, immunization can be achieved even with a non-parallel shift to the yield curve.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Curve movements are precisely the risk immunization manages; the sufficiency condition concerns them directly.

options.B: The Treasury futures example concludes that no futures position can close the BPV gap between assets and liabilities.
options.C: The example poses the hedge but leaves the required number of contracts uncomputed.

rationales.A:
Full statement: if CTD is 6.5-year T-note (BPV 56.8727, CF 0.8226), futures BPV = 56.8727/0.8226 = 69.1377 and required contracts = (117,824 - 18,819)/69.1377 = 1,432; but if CTD is 10-year T-note (BPV 81.6607, CF 0.8516), futures BPV = 95.8909 and required contracts = 1,032; difference of 400 contracts significant — must know CTD.

Correct. Per the reading: 'If the CTD security is the 6.5-year T-note shown in Exhibit 17, the futures BPV is estimated to be USD69.1377 (= 56.8727/0.8226). Then, the required number of contracts is approximately 1,432: (117,824 − 18,819)/69.1377 = 1,432. But if the CTD security is the 10-year T-note, the futures BPV is USD95.8909 (= 81.6607/0.8516). To close the money duration gap, the required number of contracts is only 1,032: (117,824 − 18,819)/95.8909 = 1,032. Clearly, the asset manager must know the CTD T-note to use in the derivatives overlay strategy. The difference of 400 futures contracts is significant.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The gap is hedgeable — the computation delivers the required contract count using the CTD's BPV and conversion factor.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The calculation is completed: futures BPV = 56.8727/0.8226 = 69.1377, contracts = (117,824 − 18,819)/69.1377.

options.A: (1) bond tender offer — buy back debt liabilities on open market at premium above market price, at price commensurate with triple-A rating to entice bondholders.
options.B: The Japanese corporation case presents a single course of action: holding the existing debt to maturity.
options.C: The case identifies no alternatives for managing the corporation's debt liabilities.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) bond tender offer — buy back debt liabilities on open market at premium above market price, at price commensurate with triple-A rating to entice bondholders; (2) cash flow matching — buys portfolio of government bonds matching coupon interest and principal on debt liabilities, external auditors would agree to accounting defeasement allowing removal from balance sheet; (3) duration matching — buys portfolio of high-quality corporate bonds matching duration, using interest rate derivatives to keep duration on target; auditors unlikely to allow accounting defeasement.

Correct. Per the case study: 'An investment bank working with the corporation offers three alternatives to accomplish the objective: 1. Bond tender offer. The corporation would buy back the debt liabilities on the open market, paying a premium above the market price... The investment bank anticipates that the tender offer would have to be at a price commensurate with a triple-A rating to entice the bondholders to sell. 2. Cash flow matching. The corporation buys a portfolio of government bonds that matches, as closely as possible, the coupon interest and principal redemptions on the debt liabilities. The investment bank is highly confident that the corporation's external auditors will agree to accounting defeasement because the purchased bonds are government securities. That agreement will allow the corporation to remove both the defeasing asset portfolio and the liabilities from the balance sheet. 3. Duration matching. The corporation buys a portfolio of high-quality corporate bonds that matches the duration of the debt liabilities. Interest rate derivatives contracts will be used to keep the duration on its target as time passes and yields change. The investment bank thinks it is very unlikely that the external auditors will allow this strategy to qualify for accounting defeasement.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Three alternatives are developed — the tender offer is one of them, alongside cash-flow matching and duration matching.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The case is built around comparing three distinct defeasance approaches.

options.A: No alternatives to the receive-fixed swap exist for closing a pension duration gap.
options.B: (1) purchased receiver swaption — confers right to buyer to enter swap as fixed-rate receiver, gain on receiver swaption as rates decline offsets losses on balance sheet, but requires paying option premium up front.
options.C: The only alternative to the swap is selling the pension's equity holdings to buy long bonds.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A purchased receiver swaption and a swaption collar are both developed as alternatives.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) purchased receiver swaption — confers right to buyer to enter swap as fixed-rate receiver, gain on receiver swaption as rates decline offsets losses on balance sheet, but requires paying option premium up front; (2) swaption collar — combination of buying receiver swaption and writing payer swaption, premium received on payer swaption offsets premium needed to buy receiver swaption.

Correct. Per the reading Summary: 'An alternative to the receive-fixed interest rate swap is a purchased receiver swaption. This swaption confers to the buyer the right to enter the swap as the fixed-rate receiver. Because of its negative duration gap (asset BPV is less than liability BPV), the typical pension plan suffers when interest rates fall and could become underfunded. The gain on the receiver swaption as rates decline offsets the losses on the balance sheet. Another alternative is a swaption collar, the combination of buying the receiver swaption and writing a payer swaption. The premium received on the payer swaption that is written offsets the premium needed to buy the receiver swaption.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The derivative alternatives — receiver swaption and collar — add hedging BPV without reallocating the asset portfolio.

options.A: short-term yields on high-quality bonds go down while long-term yields go up (1.5-year drops 25 bps, 6-year unchanged, 11.5-year up 25 bps).
options.B: In the steepening twist scenario, hedged and unhedged positions produce identical outcomes across the curve.
options.C: Exhibit 15 presents no scenario data for a steepening twist.

rationales.A:
Full statement: short-term yields on high-quality bonds go down while long-term yields go up (1.5-year drops 25 bps, 6-year unchanged, 11.5-year up 25 bps); leads to loss of EUR1,178,071 in asset portfolio as cash flow yield increases by 10.04 bps; market value of liabilities drops by only EUR835,156 because cash flow yield increases by only 7.11 bps; steepening twist to shape of yield curve is source of loss (difference EUR342,915).

Correct. Per Exhibit 15 and reading: 'This scenario is the steepening twist in which short-term yields on high-quality bonds go down while long-term yields go up. The 1.5-year yield is assumed to drop by 25 bps. The 6-year yield remains the same, and the 11.5-year yield goes up by 25 bps. These changes lead to a loss of EUR1,178,071 in the asset portfolio as the cash flow yield increases by 10.04 bps... The market value of the liabilities drops by only EUR835,156 because the cash flow yield increases by only 7.11 bps. The value of the assets goes down by more than the liabilities — the difference is EUR342,915. The steepening twist to the shape of the yield curve is the source of the loss.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The twist creates differential outcomes precisely because yield changes vary by maturity.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The scenario is specified: 1.5-year down 25 bps, 6-year unchanged, 11.5-year up 25 bps.

options.A: No formula relates asset, hedge, and liability sensitivities in interest rate hedging.
options.B: Full hedging requires only that asset BPV equal liability BPV, with no role for the hedge position.
options.C: Asset BPV × ΔAsset yields + Hedge BPV × ΔHedge yields ≈ Liability BPV × ΔLiability yields (all yield changes in basis points).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Equation 6 states the relationship explicitly across all three components.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The hedge term is integral: Asset BPV × ΔAsset yields + Hedge BPV × ΔHedge yields ≈ Liability BPV × ΔLiability yields.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Asset BPV × ΔAsset yields + Hedge BPV × ΔHedge yields ≈ Liability BPV × ΔLiability yields (all yield changes in basis points); describes immunization strategy (hedging ratio of 100%) matching changes in market value on each side of balance sheet; 'approximately equals' sign results from ignoring higher-order terms such as convexity.

Correct. Per Equation 6: 'Asset BPV × ΔAsset yields + Hedge BPV × ΔHedge yields ≈ Liability BPV × ΔLiability yields. ΔAsset yields, ΔHedge yields, and ΔLiability yields are measured in basis points. This equation describes an immunization strategy (a hedging ratio of 100%) whereby the intent is to match the changes in market value on each side of the balance sheet when yields change. Doing so entails matching the money duration of assets and liabilities. We know, however, that entities also choose to partially hedge interest rate risk by selecting a hedging ratio less than 100%. In any case, Equation 6 serves to indicate the source of the risks to LDI. The "approximately equals" sign (≈) in the equation results from ignoring higher-order terms, such as convexity.'

options.A: LDI managers face no meaningful implementation choices once the liability profile is measured.
options.B: The only implementation route in LDI is physical asset allocation; derivatives play no role.
options.C: LDI manager has fundamental choice between managing interest rate risk with asset allocation and with derivatives overlays.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The fundamental implementation choice — asset allocation versus derivatives overlay — remains after measurement.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Derivatives overlays are one of the two principal routes; the choice between them is the point.
rationales.C:
Full statement: LDI manager has fundamental choice between managing interest rate risk with asset allocation and with derivatives overlays; choice depends on thorough evaluation of risk and return trade-offs; in some circumstances, derivatives might be deemed too expensive or risky particularly regarding available collateral and cash holdings; then manager might choose to increase holdings of long-term, high-quality bonds that have high duration statistics; growth of government zero-coupon bonds (US Treasury STRIPS) facilitates that asset reallocation process.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In summary, an LDI manager has a fundamental choice between managing interest rate risk with asset allocation and with derivatives overlays. As with all financial management decisions, the choice depends on a thorough evaluation of risk and return trade-offs. In some circumstances, derivatives might be deemed too expensive or risky, particularly regarding available collateral and cash holdings. Then the manager might choose to increase holdings of long-term, high-quality bonds that have high duration statistics. The growth of government zero-coupon bonds, such as US Treasury STRIPS (Separate Trading of Registered Interest and Principal of Securities), facilitates that asset reallocation process.'

options.A: when corporation issued callable bond, effectively bought call option giving flexibility to refinance at lower cost if benchmark rates and/or credit spread narrows.
options.B: No strategy exists for a corporation seeking to defease callable debt using swaptions.
options.C: The case study addresses callable debt but proposes only open-market repurchase as the remedy.

rationales.A:
Full statement: when corporation issued callable bond, effectively bought call option giving flexibility to refinance at lower cost if benchmark rates and/or credit spread narrows; writing receiver swaption 'sells' that call option and corporation captures value of embedded call option by means of premium received; if market rates in three years higher than strike rate on swaption and yield on debt security, both options expire out of the money; if market rates fall and bond prices go up, both options in the money — corporation sells 7-year bonds (assets) and uses proceeds to call debt liabilities at par value, gain offsets loss on closing swaption.

Correct. Per the case study solution: 'The derivatives consultant should recommend that the corporation choose the fourth option and write a receiver swaption — that is, an option that gives the swaption buyer the right to enter into a swap to receive fixed and pay floating. When the corporation issued the callable bond, it effectively bought the call option, giving the corporation the flexibility to refinance at a lower cost of borrowed funds if benchmark rates and/or the corporation's credit spread narrows. Writing the receiver swaption "sells" that call option, and the corporation captures the value of the embedded call option by means of the premium received. Suppose that market rates in three years are higher than the strike rate on the swaption and the yield on the debt security. Then both options — the embedded call option in the bond liability, as well as the swaption — expire out of the money. The asset and liability both have seven years until maturity and are non-callable. Suppose instead that market rates fall and bond prices go up. Both options are now in the money. The corporation sells the seven-year bonds (the assets) and uses the proceeds to call the debt liabilities at par value. The gain on that transaction offsets the loss on closing out the swaption with the counterparty.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Writing a receiver swaption monetizes the embedded call the issuer effectively owns.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The strategy presented is the swaption sale — the issuer bought refinancing flexibility with the call and can sell an offsetting option.

options.A: Asset BPV can be measured without error whenever the liability cash flows are Type I.
options.B: in practice common to approximate asset portfolio duration using weighted average of individual durations for component bonds.
options.C: Duration approximation introduces no measurement issue in immunization practice.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Even with certain (Type I) cash flows, the common weighted-average-duration approximation introduces error.
rationales.B:
Full statement: in practice common to approximate asset portfolio duration using weighted average of individual durations for component bonds; a better approach to achieve immunization uses cash flow yield to discount future coupon and principal payments; this error is minimized when underlying yield curve is flat or when future cash flows are concentrated in flattest segment of curve.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Measurement error for asset BPV can even arise in the classic immunization strategy for Type I cash flows, which have set amounts and dates. In practice, it is common to approximate the asset portfolio duration using the weighted average of the individual durations for the component bonds. A better approach to achieve immunization, however, uses the cash flow yield to discount the future coupon and principal payments. This error is minimized when the underlying yield curve is flat or when future cash flows are concentrated in the flattest segment of the curve.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The weighted-average shortcut ignores curve shape effects — a real, acknowledged measurement error.

options.A: Hedge BPV is known exactly because futures contracts have contractually fixed sensitivities.
options.B: Derivatives overlays introduce no measurement risk into the hedging relationship.
options.C: when using derivatives overlays to immunize, Equation 4 estimates futures BPV to be BPV for CTD security divided by its conversion factor.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Futures BPV is ESTIMATED — CTD BPV divided by its conversion factor — and the CTD itself can change.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The estimation chain (CTD identification, conversion factor) is a genuine source of hedge measurement error.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when using derivatives overlays to immunize, Equation 4 estimates futures BPV to be BPV for CTD security divided by its conversion factor; a more developed calculation involving short-term rates and accrued interest could change number of contracts needed to hedge interest rate risk; although error introduced by using approximation might not be large, still can be source of underperformance in hedging strategy.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A similar problem arises in measuring hedge BPV. When we illustrated the use of derivatives overlays to immunize, we used a common approximation for the futures BPV. Equation 4 estimates it to be the BPV for the qualifying CTD security divided by its conversion factor. A more developed calculation involving short-term rates and accrued interest, however, could change the number of contracts needed to hedge the interest rate risk. Although the error introduced by using an approximation might not be large, it still can be a source of underperformance in the hedging strategy.'

options.A: Spread risk does not arise in LDI because assets and liabilities respond to the same yield curve.
options.B: LDI frameworks are immune to divergence between asset and liability discount rates.
options.C: framework rests on changes in benchmark bond yield curve (usually government bonds), but in practice ΔAsset yield and ΔLiability yield often refer to various classes of corporate bonds.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Asset yields and liability discount rates often move by DIFFERENT amounts than benchmark government yields — that divergence is spread risk.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The framework rests on the benchmark curve, but corporate-quality liability discounting and asset spreads can decouple from it.
rationales.C:
Full statement: framework rests on changes in benchmark bond yield curve (usually government bonds), but in practice ΔAsset yield and ΔLiability yield often refer to various classes of corporate bonds; risk is that respective spreads on broad index and high-quality sector do not move in unison with shift in government bond yield curve; similar spread risk present in immunizing multiple Type I liabilities.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Generally, the framework for thinking about interest rate risk rests on changes in the benchmark bond yield curve, which usually is the yield curve for government bonds. In practice, however, ΔAsset yield and ΔLiability yield often refer to various classes of corporate bonds. In the pension fund example, the fund holds a portfolio of fixed-income bonds that tracks a well-diversified index of corporate bonds that may include non-investment-grade securities. The present value of retirement benefits, however, depends on yields on high-quality corporate bonds. Therefore, a risk is that the respective spreads on the broad index and the high-quality sector do not move in unison with a shift in the government bond yield curve. A similar spread risk is present in the earlier example of immunizing multiple Type I liabilities.'

options.A: Contingent immunization carries no risk related to asset liquidity or distressed pricing.
options.B: manager may increase portfolio risk by using active management if surplus above threshold, but if losses reduce surplus to some minimum amount, positions need to be adjusted to revert to passive duration-matching portfolio of high-quality bonds.
options.C: Active positions in a contingent immunization strategy never need to be unwound.

rationales.A: Incorrect. If losses push the surplus to the trigger, positions — possibly distressed or illiquid — must be liquidated to lock in immunization.
rationales.B:
Full statement: manager may increase portfolio risk by using active management if surplus above threshold, but if losses reduce surplus to some minimum amount, positions need to be adjusted to revert to passive duration-matching portfolio of high-quality bonds; distressed assets that become hard to value, such as tranches of subprime mortgage-backed securities, also become illiquid during financial crises.

Correct. Per the reading: 'manager may increase portfolio risk by using active management. But if losses reduce the surplus to some minimum amount, the positions need to be adjusted to revert to a passive duration-matching fixed-income portfolio of high-quality bonds. Distressed assets that become hard to value, such as tranches of subprime mortgage-backed securities, also become illiquid during financial crises.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The forced unwind at the surplus floor is the strategy's defining risk; illiquid holdings make it costly.

options.A: Bond and equity markets are approximately the same size, with similar numbers of securities outstanding.
options.B: fixed-income markets are much larger and broader than equity markets, and number of fixed-income securities outstanding is vastly larger as reflected in broad market indexes.
options.C: The reading offers no comparison between fixed-income and equity market scale.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Fixed-income markets are MUCH larger and broader, with vastly more securities outstanding.
rationales.B:
Full statement: fixed-income markets are much larger and broader than equity markets, and number of fixed-income securities outstanding is vastly larger as reflected in broad market indexes; MSCI World Index (23 developed countries, 85% market cap) has ~1,600 securities; Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index (24 local currency markets) has more than 16,000 securities; those fixed-income issuers represent much wider range of borrowers than relatively narrow universe of companies issuing equity.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Fixed-income markets are much larger and broader than equity markets, and the number of fixed-income securities outstanding is vastly larger as reflected in broad market indexes. For instance, the MSCI World Index, capturing equities in 23 developed market countries and 85% of the available market capitalization in each market, consists of about 1,600 securities, whereas the Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index, covering global investment-grade debt from 24 local currency markets, consists of more than 16,000 securities. Those fixed-income issuers represent a much wider range of borrowers than the relatively narrow universe of companies issuing equity securities.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The scale comparison is drawn explicitly — a single issuer can have dozens of bonds against one common stock.

options.A: The Bloomberg Barclays US Aggregate contains no published constituent or sector data.
options.B: The US Aggregate covers a single sector: US Treasury securities only.
options.C: oldest and most widely recognized US bond market index (one of four regional aggregate benchmarks that constitute Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Constituent rules and sector weights are published and transparent.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The Aggregate spans Treasuries, agencies, corporates, and securitized sectors — one of four regional aggregates in the Global Aggregate.
rationales.C:
Full statement: oldest and most widely recognized US bond market index (one of four regional aggregate benchmarks that constitute Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index); includes US Treasuries, government agency securities, corporate bonds, mortgage-backed securities, asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities; although large number of index constituents provides means of risk diversification, in practice it is neither feasible nor cost-effective for investors to pursue full replication approach with broad fixed-income market index.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, the oldest and most widely recognized US bond market index, the Bloomberg Barclays US Aggregate Index (one of four regional aggregate benchmarks that constitute the Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index), includes US Treasuries, government agency securities, corporate bonds, mortgage-backed securities, asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. Although the large number of index constituents provides a means of risk diversification, in practice it is neither feasible nor cost-effective for investors to pursue a full replication approach with a broad fixed-income market index.'

options.A: as of October 2020, Royal Dutch Shell had 57 bonds outstanding across four currencies (some both fixed and floating rate), range of maturities under a year to bonds maturing in 2052.
options.B: Exhibit 24 provides no issuer-level data comparing debt and equity securities outstanding.
options.C: The reading makes no comparison of security counts between debt and equity for any issuer.

rationales.A:
Full statement: as of October 2020, Royal Dutch Shell had 57 bonds outstanding across four currencies (some both fixed and floating rate), range of maturities under a year to bonds maturing in 2052; BHP Billiton 22 bonds vs 1 common; Johnson & Johnson 37 vs 1; Ford Motor Company 104 fixed-income securities vs 2 common equity + 2 preferred equity; existence of many debt securities for a particular issuer suggests many near substitutes may exist for investor seeking to pursue enhanced index strategy.

Correct. Per Exhibit 24 (Debt and Equity Securities Outstanding for Select Issuers): 'Royal Dutch Shell PLC: 57 Fixed-Income, 1 Common Equity, 0 Preferred. BHP Billiton Limited: 22, 1, 0. Johnson & Johnson: 37, 1, 0. Ford Motor Company: 104, 2, 2.' And: 'As of October 2020, Royal Dutch Shell had 57 bonds outstanding across four currencies, some of which were both fixed and floating rate, with a range of maturities from under a year to bonds maturing in 2052. The existence of many debt securities for a particular issuer suggests that many near substitutes may exist for an investor seeking to pursue an enhanced index strategy.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The exhibit details a real issuer's outstanding bonds by currency, coupon type, and maturity.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Royal Dutch Shell's 57 bonds across four currencies against its common equity is the illustration.

options.A: Fixed-income trading is predominantly exchange-based with central limit order books like equities.
options.B: largely over-the-counter markets that rely on broker/dealers as principals to trade using quote-based execution process rather than order-based trading systems common in equity markets.
options.C: Bond and equity market structures are identical in venue and execution process.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Bond markets are largely OTC, quote-driven through broker/dealer principals — with Basel III raising dealers' inventory costs.
rationales.B:
Full statement: largely over-the-counter markets that rely on broker/dealers as principals to trade using quote-based execution process rather than order-based trading systems common in equity markets; rising cost of maintaining risk-weighted assets on dealer balance sheets because of Basel III capital requirements has had adverse effect on fixed-income trading and liquidity; broker/dealers reduced bond inventories; dealers less willing to support larger 'block' trades preferring smaller trade sizes; significant decline in proprietary trading among dealers has had greater pricing effect on less liquid 'off-the-run' bonds.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Unlike equity securities, which trade primarily over an exchange, fixed-income markets are largely over-the-counter markets that rely on broker/dealers as principals to trade in these securities using a quote-based execution process rather than the order-based trading systems common in equity markets. The rising cost of maintaining risk-weighted assets on dealer balance sheets because of Basel III capital requirements has had an adverse effect on fixed-income trading and liquidity. Broker/dealers have reduced bond inventories because of higher capital costs. With lower trading inventories, dealers have both a limited appetite to facilitate trading at narrow bid–offer spreads and are less willing to support larger "block" trades, preferring execution in smaller trade sizes. Finally, a significant decline in proprietary trading among dealers has had a greater pricing effect on less liquid or "off-the-run" bonds.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Quote-based OTC execution versus order-based exchange trading is the structural difference.

options.A: No US system exists for reporting over-the-counter bond transactions.
options.B: US corporate bond trades are reported voluntarily, at each dealer's discretion.
options.C: US Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine — regulators developed system that facilitates mandatory reporting of over-the-counter transactions in eligible fixed-income securities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. TRACE — the Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine — is that system.
rationales.B: Incorrect. TRACE reporting is MANDATORY for covered OTC transactions.
rationales.C:
Full statement: US Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine — regulators developed system that facilitates mandatory reporting of over-the-counter transactions in eligible fixed-income securities; all broker/dealers that are FINRA (Financial Industry Regulatory Authority) member firms must report corporate bond transactions within 15 minutes of occurrence; vast majority of fixed-income securities either do not trade at all or trade only a few times during the year; average trade size in dollar terms in US investment-grade bond market is roughly 70 times size of average stock trade.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Fixed-income trading in many markets is difficult to track. In some markets, regulators developed systems that facilitate mandatory reporting of over-the-counter transactions in eligible fixed-income securities, such as the US Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine (TRACE) system. All broker/dealers that are Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) member firms must report corporate bond transactions within 15 minutes of occurrence. It is important to note the distinct nature of fixed-income trading versus equities. The vast majority of fixed-income securities either do not trade at all or trade only a few times during the year. Only a small fraction trade every business day, according to MarketAxess, a leading electronic trading provider. It is also important to note that the average trade size in dollar terms in the US investment-grade bond market is roughly 70 times the size of the average stock trade.'

options.A: refers to change in a non-Treasury security's price given a widening or narrowing of the spread compared with the benchmark.
options.B: Spread duration is not a recognized fixed-income risk measure.
options.C: Spread duration measures a Treasury security's sensitivity to changes in its own benchmark yield.

rationales.A:
Full statement: refers to change in a non-Treasury security's price given a widening or narrowing of the spread compared with the benchmark; for non-government fixed-income securities, we separate yield to maturity into benchmark yield (typically most recently issued or on-the-run government bond with closest time to maturity) and a spread reflecting difference between benchmark yield and security-specific yield; matching relative quality between portfolio and fixed-income index will minimize this risk.

Correct. Per the reading: 'the risk that a bond's price will change in response to an idiosyncratic rate move rather than an overall market yield change is known as spread risk. For non-government fixed-income securities, we separate the yield to maturity into a benchmark yield (typically the most recently issued or on-the-run government bond with the closest time to maturity) and a spread reflecting the difference between the benchmark yield and the security-specific yield. Spread duration refers to the change in a non-Treasury security's price given a widening or narrowing of the spread compared with the benchmark. Matching the relative quality between the portfolio and the fixed-income index will minimize this risk.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. It is the standard measure of price sensitivity to spread changes for non-Treasury securities.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The concept applies to NON-Treasury securities and isolates the spread component versus the benchmark.

options.A: Full index replication is chosen without any supporting rationale, reflecting institutional habit rather than a considered view on active management or cost structures.
options.B: index replication done based on expectation that (i) active manager cannot consistently outperform index on risk-adjusted basis, (ii) investor cannot identify skilled manager in advance, or (iii) investor is not prepared to go through periods of underperformance.
options.C: Index replication serves no identifiable investor purpose relative to active management, since it guarantees benchmark-matching gross returns while charging comparable fees.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The rationale is explicit — skepticism of consistent risk-adjusted active outperformance, cost minimization, and benchmark-relative accountability.
rationales.B:
Full statement: index replication done based on expectation that (i) active manager cannot consistently outperform index on risk-adjusted basis, (ii) investor cannot identify skilled manager in advance, or (iii) investor is not prepared to go through periods of underperformance; in practice investors that wish to have exposure to index rely on one of many ETFs that exist in this space.

Correct. Per the reading: 'expectation that (i) an active manager cannot consistently outperform the index on a risk-adjusted basis, (ii) the investor cannot identify a skilled manager in advance, or (iii) the investor is not prepared to go through periods of underperformance. Initial index replication does not require manager analysis but rather involves sourcing a wide range of securities in exact proportion to the index, many of which may be thinly traded.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Low cost and reliable benchmark tracking are the purposes replication serves.

options.A: Enhanced indexing through stratified sampling has no defined methodology.
options.B: goal is to mirror most important index characteristics and still closely track index performance over time while purchasing fewer securities.
options.C: Stratified sampling in bond indexing serves no purpose beyond reducing reporting burden.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The method is defined: match the index's most important characteristics with fewer securities.
rationales.B:
Full statement: goal is to mirror most important index characteristics and still closely track index performance over time while purchasing fewer securities; also called cell approach; first, each cell or significant index portfolio characteristic identified and mapped to current index; second, portfolio manager identifies subset of bonds or bond-linked exposures (derivatives) with characteristics corresponding to index; finally, positions in each cell adjusted over time given changes to underlying index vs existing portfolio positions; e.g., index contains 1,000 fixed-income securities, 10% AAA rated → manager might choose 5-10 AAA rated securities within cell to mimic performance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Many limitations of the full replication approach are addressed by an enhanced indexing strategy. This approach's goal is to mirror the most important index characteristics and still closely track index performance over time while purchasing fewer securities. This general approach is referred to as a stratified sampling or cell approach to indexing. First, each cell or significant index portfolio characteristic is identified and mapped to the current index. Second, the fixed-income portfolio manager identifies a subset of bonds or bond-linked exposures, such as derivatives, with characteristics that correspond to the index. Finally, the positions in each cell are adjusted over time given changes to the underlying index versus existing portfolio positions. For example, say a fixed-income index contains 1,000 fixed-income securities, 10% of which are AAA rated. The portfolio manager might choose 5–10 AAA rated securities within a cell to mimic the performance of the AAA rated bonds within the index.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The purpose is close tracking at lower cost — full bond-index replication is often impractical.

options.A: ESG incorporation in bond indexes has no identifiable components.
options.B: Bond index ESG methodologies consist solely of overweighting green bond issues.
options.C: (1) business involvement screen excludes issuers involved in business lines/activities such as alcohol, gambling, tobacco, adult entertainment, nuclear power, and firearms.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Two components are identified — business-involvement screens and ESG ratings-based selection.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Green bond tilts are not the framework; exclusionary screens plus ratings integration are.
rationales.C:
Full statement: (1) business involvement screen excludes issuers involved in business lines/activities such as alcohol, gambling, tobacco, adult entertainment, nuclear power, and firearms; (2) ESG rating, provided by numerous third-party companies applied (MSCI provides ratings on 'AAA to CCC' scale using rules-based methodology according to companies' exposure to ESG risks and how well they are managed relative to peers); when building sustainable index, Bloomberg applies such rules then further filters constituents for business involvement and minimum MSCI rating (BBB); given proliferation of ESG data providers, differences across methodologies and ratings.

Correct. Per the reading: 'There are two main components to incorporating ESG factors in an index. First, a business involvement screen excludes issuers involved in business lines or such activities as alcohol, gambling, tobacco, adult entertainment, nuclear power, and firearms. Second, an ESG rating, provided by one of numerous third-party companies, is applied. MSCI, one such company, provides ratings on an "AAA to CCC" scale using a rules-based methodology according to the companies' exposure to ESG risks and how well they are managed relative to peers. When building a sustainable index, for example, Bloomberg will apply such rules as those previously described and then further filter the constituents for business involvement and an MSCI minimum rating requirement of BBB. Given the proliferation of ESG data providers, there are differences across methodologies and ratings.'

options.A: (1) lower cost enhancements — minimizing fund expenses or introducing more competitive trading process to reduce bid-offer cost of trading.
options.B: Volpert identifies no strategies for enhancing indexed bond portfolio returns.
options.C: The only enhancement Volpert describes is adding equity exposure to the bond portfolio.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) lower cost enhancements — minimizing fund expenses or introducing more competitive trading process to reduce bid-offer cost of trading; (2) issue selection enhancements — use of bond valuation models to identify specific issues that are undervalued or 'cheap' to their implied value; (3) yield curve enhancements — use of analytical models to gauge relative value across term structure of interest rates; (4) sector/quality enhancements — overweighting specific bond and credit sectors across business cycle; (5) call exposure enhancements — significant yield changes affect bond performance especially when a bond shifts from trading to maturity to trading to earlier call date.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Volpert (2012) outlines a number of enhancement strategies available to portfolio managers seeking to reduce the component of tracking error associated with the expenses and transaction costs of portfolio management as follows: Lower cost enhancements... Issue selection enhancements... Yield curve enhancements... Sector/quality enhancements. This strategy involves overweighting specific bond and credit sectors across the business cycle to enhance returns... Call exposure enhancements. Because effective duration is a sufficient risk measure only for relatively small rate changes, anticipated larger yield changes may affect bond performance significantly, especially when a bond shifts from trading to maturity to trading to an earlier call date... Callable fixed-income securities (priced on a yield-to-call basis) trading above par tend to be less price sensitive for a given effective duration than those priced on a yield-to-maturity basis, suggesting a manager should use metrics other than effective duration in this case when changing exposure.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Several enhancement categories are catalogued, beginning with lower-cost enhancements.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The enhancements are fixed-income-native: cost reduction, issue selection, yield curve positioning, sector/quality positioning, call exposure positioning.

options.A: Callable securities are always more price sensitive than option-free bonds of the same duration.
options.B: callable fixed-income securities (priced on yield-to-call basis) trading above par tend to be less price sensitive for a given effective duration than those priced on yield-to-maturity basis.
options.C: Call features have no effect on a bond's price sensitivity profile.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Premium callables priced to call tend to be LESS price sensitive for a given effective duration.
rationales.B:
Full statement: callable fixed-income securities (priced on yield-to-call basis) trading above par tend to be less price sensitive for a given effective duration than those priced on yield-to-maturity basis; suggests manager should use metrics other than effective duration in this case when changing exposure; e.g., anticipated drop in yields might cause callable bond to shift from being priced on yield-to-maturity basis to yield-to-call basis.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, an anticipated drop in yields might cause a callable bond to shift from being priced on a yield-to-maturity basis to a yield-to-call basis. Callable fixed-income securities (priced on a yield-to-call basis) trading above par tend to be less price sensitive for a given effective duration than those priced on a yield-to-maturity basis, suggesting a manager should use metrics other than effective duration in this case when changing exposure.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The call feature compresses upside price response — the yield-to-call pricing basis is exactly why.

options.A: The three Adelaide Super funds carry identical risk profiles.
options.B: The case study provides no basis for distinguishing the funds' risk levels.
options.C: Paisley Fixed-Interest Fund represents lowest risk of three (half in short-term securities via AusBond Bank Bill Index + lack of active management, buy-and-hold) suitable for investor near retirement.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The funds differ materially in duration, credit, and management style.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Paisley emerges as lowest risk — half in short-term securities via the AusBond Bank Bill Index, without active management risk.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Paisley Fixed-Interest Fund represents lowest risk of three (half in short-term securities via AusBond Bank Bill Index + lack of active management, buy-and-hold) suitable for investor near retirement; Dundee Australian Fixed-Income Fund medium risk profile (composite benchmark, enhanced indexing approach, match index duration but add value through fundamental strategies) best for middle-aged worker; Newcastleton Australian Bond Fund highest risk (actively managed with mandate to take positions in duration and credit that deviate from index) appropriate for younger worker.

Correct. Per the case study solution: 'The Paisley Fixed-Interest Fund represents the lowest risk of the three fund choices, given both its choice of underlying bond index (half of which is in short-term securities) and lack of active management strategies. The Paisley Fund could be a suitable choice for an investor near retirement who is seeking income with a minimum risk profile. The Dundee Fund represents a medium risk profile given the choice of the composite benchmark and suggests an enhanced approach to indexing. This fund may be the best choice for a middle-aged worker seeking to add a fixed-income component with moderate risk to his portfolio. The Newcastleton Fund has the highest risk of the three choices and is an example of an actively managed fund that has a mandate to take positions in primary risk factors, such as duration and credit, that deviate from those of the index to generate excess return. This fund could be an appropriate choice for a younger worker who is seeking exposure to fixed income but willing to accommodate higher risk.'

options.A: Benchmark selection precedes and determines the investor's goals and objectives.
options.B: No structured process links investor objectives to benchmark choice.
options.C: starts with clear delineation of portfolio manager's investment goals and objectives (protection of funds against inflation, broad market replication, predictable returns within acceptable risk parameters, or maximum absolute returns).

rationales.A: Incorrect. The sequence runs the other way — goals and objectives are delineated FIRST, then the benchmark follows.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The process is structured: objectives (inflation protection, market returns, liability funding) drive the benchmark decision.
rationales.C:
Full statement: starts with clear delineation of portfolio manager's investment goals and objectives (protection of funds against inflation, broad market replication, predictable returns within acceptable risk parameters, or maximum absolute returns); manager must agree on investment policy with asset owners/beneficiaries/other constituents outlining return objectives, risk tolerance, and constraints; strategic asset allocation targeting specific weightings for each permissible asset class results from process; tactical asset allocation range often provides investment manager some short-term flexibility to deviate from these weightings.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The asset allocation process starts with a clear delineation of the portfolio manager's investment goals and objectives. Examples of such goals might include the protection of funds (especially against inflation), broad market replication, predictable returns within acceptable risk parameters, or maximum absolute returns through opportunistic means. The manager must agree on an investment policy with the asset owners, beneficiaries, and other constituents outlining return objectives, risk tolerance, and constraints to narrow choices available in the broader capital markets to meet these objectives. Recall that a strategic asset allocation targeting specific weightings for each permissible asset class is the result of this process, while a tactical asset allocation range often provides the investment manager some short-term flexibility to deviate from these weightings in response to anticipated market changes.'

options.A: clear, transparent rules for security inclusion and weighting, investability, daily valuation and availability of past returns, and turnover.
options.B: Bond benchmark selection is unconstrained by any criteria; any published index qualifies.
options.C: The only requirement for a benchmark index is that it contain more than 100 securities.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'Benchmark selection must factor in the broad range of issuers and characteristics available in the fixed-income markets. In general, the use of an index as a widely accepted benchmark requires clear, transparent rules for security inclusion and weighting, investability, daily valuation and availability of past returns, and turnover.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Clear rules, investability, daily valuation, availability of history, and turnover data are required criteria.
rationales.C: Incorrect. No security-count threshold exists; rule transparency and investability are the tests.

options.A: desired duration profile may be considered portfolio 'beta' with targeted duration equal to investor's preferred duration exposure.
options.B: Fixed-income portfolios have no analog to the equity beta concept.
options.C: The fixed-income beta is credit rating rather than any duration measure.

rationales.A:
Full statement: desired duration profile may be considered portfolio 'beta' with targeted duration equal to investor's preferred duration exposure; once parameters clear, investors may combine several well-defined sub-benchmark categories into overall benchmark; examples of sub-benchmark categories: Treasuries (or domestic sovereign bonds), US agencies or other asset-backed securities, corporate bonds, high-yield bonds, bank loans, developed markets global debt, or emerging markets debt.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The dynamics of fixed-income markets require investors to more actively understand and define their underlying duration preferences as well as a desired risk and return profile within their fixed-income allocation when conducting benchmark selection. Expressed differently, the desired duration profile may be considered the portfolio "beta," with the targeted duration equal to an investor's preferred duration exposure. Once these parameters are clear, investors may wish to combine several well-defined sub-benchmark categories into an overall benchmark. Examples of sub-benchmark categories might include Treasuries (or domestic sovereign bonds), US agencies or other asset-backed securities, corporate bonds, high-yield bonds, bank loans, developed markets global debt, or emerging markets debt.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The targeted duration profile plays the beta role — the portfolio's systematic exposure choice.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Duration, not rating, is the primary systematic exposure treated as the portfolio's 'beta'.

options.A: Investor-specific duration preferences play no role in fixed-income benchmark choice.
options.B: Duration preference is a minor stylistic factor without structural grounding.
options.C: choosing a fixed-income benchmark is unique in that investor usually has some degree of fixed-income exposure embedded within its asset/liability portfolio as outlined in immunization and liability-driven investing examples.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Embedded asset/liability duration makes the investor's implicit or explicit duration preference central to the choice.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The preference is structural — fixed-income exposure already embedded in the investor's balance sheet shapes the appropriate benchmark.
rationales.C:
Full statement: choosing a fixed-income benchmark is unique in that investor usually has some degree of fixed-income exposure embedded within its asset/liability portfolio as outlined in immunization and liability-driven investing examples; investment manager must therefore consider these implicit or explicit duration preferences when choosing fixed-income benchmark.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Choosing a fixed-income benchmark is unique, however, in that the investor usually has some degree of fixed-income exposure embedded within its asset/liability portfolio, as outlined in the foregoing immunization and liability-driven investing examples. The investment manager must therefore consider these implicit or explicit duration preferences when choosing a fixed-income benchmark.'

options.A: highly risk-averse investor sensitive to fluctuations in portfolio value would prefer index with short or intermediate duration with limited credit risk to limit market value risk.
options.B: The highly risk-averse investor in the case study prefers the longest-duration index available.
options.C: The case study finds risk aversion irrelevant to index preference.

rationales.A:
Full statement: highly risk-averse investor sensitive to fluctuations in portfolio value would prefer index with short or intermediate duration with limited credit risk to limit market value risk; of alternatives listed, S&P Eurozone Sovereign Bond 1-3 Years Index or FTSE 1-3 Years Pfandbrief Index (given high credit quality of covered bonds) would be appropriate.

Correct. Per case study solution: 'Given this investor's high degree of risk aversion, an index with short or intermediate duration with limited credit risk would be most appropriate to limit market value risk. Of the alternatives listed, the S&P Eurozone Sovereign Bond 1–3 Years Index or the FTSE 1–3 Years Pfandbrief Index (given the high credit quality of covered bonds) would be appropriate choices.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Sensitivity to value fluctuations points to SHORT or intermediate duration with strong credit quality.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Risk aversion drives the recommendation directly — limited duration and high quality.

options.A: Correlations between fixed-income indexes are driven purely by the dividend yields of the underlying issuers' equities.
options.B: the interest rate component (duration) and by geography — investment-grade securities in developed economies are highly correlated with sovereign yield curve changes.
options.C: Correlations between fixed-income indexes are determined only by credit rating, with duration and geography playing no role.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Dividend yield is an equity attribute; the reading's FI correlation drivers are the interest rate (duration) component and geography.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the interest rate component (duration) and by geography — investment-grade securities in developed economies are highly correlated with sovereign yield curve changes; below-investment-grade securities are affected more by spread changes and often show stronger correlations with equity markets.

Correct. The reading states within the FI asset class, correlations between FI indexes are driven largely by the interest rate component (duration) and by geography. In developed economies, investment-grade securities with a low probability of default are highly correlated with interest rate changes in the sovereign yield curve. Below-investment-grade securities are affected more by changes in spread than by changes in general interest rates and often exhibit stronger correlations with equity markets. Correlations between interest rates and spreads can often be negative.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Rating matters at the security level, but index correlations trace to shared duration exposure and regional rate linkages.

options.A: Enhanced indexing matches the benchmark in every dimension, holding all constituents at exact index weights with zero deviation.
options.B: Modest outperformance (generally 20-30 bps) of benchmark, active risk kept low (typically around 50 bps or lower), small deviations from underlying benchmark, most primary risk factors closely matched (particularly duration), slightly higher turnover than underlying benchmark.
options.C: Enhanced indexing targets outperformance of 50 bps or more through large deviations from benchmark factor exposures and sector weights.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Exact full matching is PURE indexing; enhanced indexing permits small deviations to seek modest outperformance.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Exhibit 6, enhanced indexing objective is modest outperformance (generally 20-30 bps) of benchmark while active risk is kept low (typically around 50 bps or lower). Portfolio weights show small deviations from underlying benchmark. Most primary risk factors are closely matched (particularly duration). Turnover is slightly higher than underlying benchmark. This contrasts with pure indexing (zero target active return and risk) and active management (50+ bps outperformance with larger deviations).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Large factor deviations and 50+ bps targets describe ACTIVE management; enhanced indexing targets roughly 20-30 bps with active risk near 50 bps or lower.

options.A: Effective duration differs from modified duration because it ignores yield changes entirely, measuring only the passage of time.
options.B: effective duration measures the sensitivity of the bond's price to a change in the benchmark yield curve (using a parallel shift), and is essential for measuring interest rate risk of complex bonds where future cash flows are uncertain (e.g., bonds with embedded options).
options.C: Effective duration differs from modified duration because it cannot be computed for bonds with embedded options.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Effective duration measures price response to benchmark curve shifts — yield changes are its very input.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading defines effective duration as the sensitivity of a bond's price to a change in a benchmark yield curve (i.e., using a parallel shift in the benchmark yield curve, ΔCurve). Effective duration is essential to the measurement of the interest rate risk of a complex bond where future cash flows are uncertain — such as callable/putable bonds and MBS with embedded options. The formula: EffDur = [(PV_) − (PV+)] / [2 × ΔCurve × PV_0].
rationales.C: Incorrect. The opposite: effective duration is essential precisely FOR bonds whose cash flows change with rates (embedded options, MBS).

options.A: PVBP is computed by multiplying money duration by 100, converting it to a percentage-of-par measure.
options.B: PVBP 'scales' money duration so it can be interpreted as money gained or lost for each basis point change in the reference interest rate.
options.C: PVBP has no relationship to money duration; the two measures answer unrelated questions.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The scaling is by 0.0001 (one basis point), not a multiplication by 100.
rationales.B:
Full statement: PVBP 'scales' money duration so it can be interpreted as money gained or lost for each basis point change in the reference interest rate — a related statistic, sometimes called 'basis point value' (BPV), is the money duration times 0.0001 (1 bp).

Correct. The reading defines PVBP as an estimate of the change in a bond's price given a 1 bp change in yield to maturity. PVBP 'scales' money duration so that it can be interpreted as money gained or lost for each basis point change in the reference interest rate. Also referred to in North America as the 'dollar value of an 0.01' (DV01) — calibrated to a bond's par value of 100. Related statistic BPV = money duration × 0.0001. The terms PVBP and DV01 are used interchangeably.
rationales.C: Incorrect. PVBP IS scaled money duration — money gained or lost per one-basis-point change in the reference rate.

options.A: For option-free zero-coupon bonds, Macaulay durations decrease as maturity lengthens, and convexity is unrelated to duration.
options.B: For zeros, convexity is inversely proportional to duration, so longer-duration zeros carry systematically less convexity.
options.C: Macaulay durations increase *linearly* with maturity (a 30-year zero has 3× the duration of a 10-year zero), and convexity is approximately proportional to duration squared (a 30-year zero has about 9× the convexity of a 10-year zero).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Duration INCREASES with maturity for zeros — linearly, so a 30-year zero has three times the duration of a 10-year zero.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The relationship is direct — convexity is approximately proportional to duration squared, not inverse.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading identifies these helpful heuristics: for zero-coupon (option-free) bonds, Macaulay durations increase linearly with maturity — a 30-year zero has 3× the duration of a 10-year zero. Convexity is approximately proportional to duration squared — a 30-year zero has about 9× (3²) the convexity of a 10-year zero. Additionally, coupon-paying bonds have more convexity than zero-coupon bonds of the same duration (a 30-year coupon-paying bond with duration ~18 years has more convexity than an 18-year zero). A zero-coupon bond has the lowest convexity of all bonds of a given duration.

options.A: Duration times spread modifies spread duration to eliminate the impact of duration from the credit risk measure entirely.
options.B: incorporate the empirical observation that spread changes across the credit spectrum tend to occur on a proportional percentage basis rather than absolute bp basis.
options.C: Duration times spread is a pure interest rate risk measure with no connection to credit spreads.

rationales.A: Incorrect. DTS builds ON duration — it scales spread duration by the spread level rather than removing duration.
rationales.B:
Full statement: incorporate the empirical observation that spread changes across the credit spectrum tend to occur on a proportional percentage basis rather than absolute bp basis — DTS weights the spread duration by a factor equal to the current credit spread, increasing the magnitude of expected price changes for a given change in spread.

Correct. The reading defines DTS as a modification of the spread duration definition to incorporate the empirical observation that spread changes across the credit spectrum tend to occur on a proportional percentage basis rather than being based on absolute basis point changes. This measure weights the spread duration by a factor equal to the current credit spread, increasing the magnitude of expected price changes for a given change in spread. Portfolio managers often use DTS when adjusting credit exposure by rating (e.g., reducing A-rated names and increasing BBB-rated credits).
rationales.C: Incorrect. DTS is a CREDIT-risk measure reflecting that spread changes tend to be proportional (percentage) rather than absolute.

options.A: Portfolio dispersion is defined as the mean of the times to receipt of the portfolio's cash flows.
options.B: Portfolio dispersion is the standard deviation of the portfolio's periodic total returns.
options.C: the variance of the times to receipt of cash flows with respect to the duration — measures the extent to which payments are spread out around the duration.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The weighted MEAN of cash-flow times is duration; dispersion is the variance around that mean.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Dispersion is a cash-flow TIMING statistic, not a return-volatility statistic.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the variance of the times to receipt of cash flows with respect to the duration — measures the extent to which payments are spread out around the duration; higher cash flow dispersion leads to an increase in convexity, and is used in measuring interest rate immunization for liabilities.

Correct. The reading defines portfolio dispersion as capturing the variance of the times to receipt of cash flows with respect to the duration. Whereas Macaulay duration is the weighted average of the times to receipt of cash flows, dispersion is the weighted variance. It measures the extent to which the payments are spread out around the duration. Convexity is affected by the dispersion of cash flows — higher cash flow dispersion leads to an increase in convexity. It is used in measuring interest rate immunization for liabilities.

options.A: Bid-ask spreads on bonds are determined purely by the coupon rate, with higher coupons commanding wider spreads.
options.B: Bid-ask spreads are identical across all bonds because dealers quote a standardized market-wide spread.
options.C: riskiness (riskier bonds have higher spreads due to dealers' aversion to holding them), complexity (embedded options widen spreads), and creditworthiness (bonds of large high-credit-quality corporations with many outstanding issues have the lowest spreads among corporates).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Coupon is not the driver; riskiness, complexity, and credit quality shape dealers' quoted spreads.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Spreads vary widely — dealers charge more to hold riskier, more complex, less liquid paper.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading states several factors determine bid-ask spread: (1) riskier bonds often have higher bid-ask spreads because of dealers' aversion to hold them (dealers must finance inventories and want to cover both costs and profit); (2) bond complexity — plain vanilla corporate bonds normally have lower spreads than corporate bonds with non-standard/complex features (e.g., embedded options); (3) creditworthiness — bonds of large, high-credit-quality corporations with many outstanding issues are the most liquid among corporate bonds and thus have relatively low bid-ask spreads. Government bond spreads are lowest of all.

options.A: Dealer inventories have no impact on quoted spreads because dealers hedge all inventory instantly.
options.B: less liquid bonds are likely to remain in dealer inventory *longer* than liquid bonds, and dealers therefore incur higher costs of financing and holding those bonds.
options.C: Less liquid bonds always trade with narrower spreads because dealers discount them to move inventory faster.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Inventory dynamics drive quotes — financing and holding costs accumulate while positions sit.
rationales.B:
Full statement: less liquid bonds are likely to remain in dealer inventory *longer* than liquid bonds, and dealers therefore incur higher costs of financing and holding those bonds — they seek to cover costs and profit through the bid-ask spread, so spreads will be higher for illiquid bonds.

Correct. The reading states bond dealers often carry an inventory of bonds because buy and sell orders don't arrive simultaneously. A dealer is not certain how long bonds will remain in inventory. Less liquid bonds are likely to remain in inventory longer than liquid bonds. A dealer provides bid-ask quotes on bonds of its choice. Some illiquid bonds won't have quotes, particularly bid quotes. Because dealers must finance their inventories, they incur costs in obtaining funding and holding those bonds. Dealers cover their costs and make a profit through the bid-ask spread — the spread will be higher for illiquid bonds that are likely to remain in inventory longer.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The relationship is opposite: longer expected inventory residence means WIDER spreads to cover funding costs.

options.A: The domestic-currency return on a foreign bond is the product of the two returns: R_DC = R_FC × R_FX.
options.B: The domestic-currency return is the simple sum of the foreign return and the currency return: R_DC = R_FC + R_FX.
options.C: R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1, where R_DC and R_FC are domestic and foreign currency returns and R_FX is the percentage change of the domestic currency versus the foreign currency.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Simple multiplication of the returns is not the relationship; the returns compound as (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The sum ignores the cross-term R_FC × R_FX; compounding is the accurate formulation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1, where R_DC and R_FC are domestic and foreign currency returns and R_FX is the percentage change of the domestic currency versus the foreign currency; for a portfolio: R_DC = Σ ω_i (1 + R_FC,i)(1 + R_FX,i) − 1.

Correct. The reading states R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1 for a single asset, or R_DC = Σ ω_i (1 + R_FC,i)(1 + R_FX,i) − 1 for a portfolio, where R_DC and R_FC are domestic and foreign currency returns as a percentage, R_FX is the percentage change of the domestic currency versus the foreign currency, and ω_i are portfolio weights. This is a compound (not simple) relationship.

options.A: The leveraged portfolio return is the product of the invested return and the borrowing rate: r_P = r_I × r_B.
options.B: The leveraged portfolio return is simply the invested return less the borrowing rate: r_P = r_I − r_B.
options.C: r_P = r_I + (V_B / V_E) × (r_I − r_B), where r_P is leveraged portfolio return, r_I is return on invested funds, r_B is borrowing rate, V_B is borrowed funds, V_E is equity.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Multiplying by the borrowing rate has no economic meaning here.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Simple subtraction omits the leverage multiplier V_B/V_E applied to the spread (r_I − r_B).
rationales.C: Correct. The reading decomposes r_P = r_I + (V_B / V_E) × (r_I − r_B). This shows the leveraged return equals return on invested funds plus a leverage-dependent term. If r_I > r_B (investment return exceeds borrowing rate), the second term is positive and leverage boosts return. If r_I < r_B, leverage reduces return. The degree of enhancement or drag is proportional to leverage (V_B/V_E) and the spread (r_I − r_B).

options.A: an important source of short-term financing where a security owner agrees to sell a security for a specific cash amount while simultaneously agreeing to repurchase the security at a specified future date and price.
options.B: A repurchase agreement is an outright securities transfer that carries no obligation to repurchase the security.
options.C: A repurchase agreement is an equity investment vehicle for gaining diversified stock exposure.

rationales.A:
Full statement: an important source of short-term financing where a security owner agrees to sell a security for a specific cash amount while simultaneously agreeing to repurchase the security at a specified future date and price — effectively a collateralized loan.

Correct. The reading describes repos as important sources of short-term financing for FI security dealers and other financial institutions, evidenced by trillions of dollars annually. In a repo, a security owner agrees to sell a security for a specific cash amount while simultaneously agreeing to repurchase the security at a specified future date (typically one day later) and price. Repos are effectively collateralized loans. From the borrower's standpoint the transaction is a repo; from the lender's standpoint (e.g., money market fund) it's a reverse repo.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The simultaneous agreement to repurchase at a specified price and date is the defining feature.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Repos are collateralized short-term DEBT financing, not equity vehicles.

options.A: The rebate rate in securities lending is always exactly zero by market convention.
options.B: The rebate rate is always positive regardless of how strong the borrowing demand for the security becomes.
options.C: the portion of the collateral earnings rate that is repaid to the security borrower by the security lender: Rebate rate = Collateral earnings rate − Security lending rate.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The rebate equals collateral earnings minus the security lending rate — normally positive, not zero.
rationales.B: Incorrect. For hard-to-borrow securities in extreme demand, the rebate can turn NEGATIVE.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the portion of the collateral earnings rate that is repaid to the security borrower by the security lender: Rebate rate = Collateral earnings rate − Security lending rate; when securities are difficult to borrow (high demand), the rebate rate may be negative.

Correct. The reading states when bonds are posted as collateral, the income earned on the collateral usually exceeds the security lending rate — the security lender (in possession of the bonds) usually repays the security borrower a portion of the interest earned on the bond collateral. The rebate rate = collateral earnings rate − security lending rate. When securities are difficult to borrow (typically because there is high demand to short), the rebate rate may be negative — the fee for borrowing the securities exceeds the return on the collateral. In this case, the security borrower pays a fee in addition to foregoing the collateral interest.

options.A: The manager should purchase additional US Treasuries with available cash to express both views simultaneously.
options.B: selling US Treasury futures to reduce interest rate duration (retaining spread duration on corporate bonds) and entering a receive-fixed, pay-floating swap on Brazilian rates for the bullish EM view.
options.C: The manager should convert the entire fund to cash, exiting both the rate and spread exposures.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Buying Treasuries INCREASES the rate exposure the bearish rate view says to cut.
rationales.B:
Full statement: selling US Treasury futures to reduce interest rate duration (retaining spread duration on corporate bonds) and entering a receive-fixed, pay-floating swap on Brazilian rates for the bullish EM view — both are leveraged since only counterparty collateral is required, but incorrect views on either position magnify losses.

Correct. Per the reading's Arturo example: bearish on US Treasury rates requires reducing duration — sell US Treasury futures whose notional and duration offset the corporate bond duration to reach new target duration; this retains spread duration exposure to corporates while shedding interest rate risk. Bullish on Brazilian rates: enter a receive-fixed, pay-floating swap on Brazilian rates — effectively creates a long fixed-rate bond position that will appreciate if Brazilian rates fall. Both positions are leveraged (only counterparty collateral used). Risk: incorrect views magnify losses, potentially requiring position closure at inopportune times.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Full liquidation forfeits the bullish EM spread view and abandons the mandate; derivatives express both views without wholesale turnover.

options.A: Zero-coupon bonds are never taxed until maturity in any jurisdiction, deferring the entire tax liability.
options.B: imputed interest that is taxed throughout the bond's life may be calculated, ensuring tax is paid over the bond's life and the return is not taxed entirely as a capital gain.
options.C: The entire return on a zero-coupon bond is taxed as a capital gain at the moment of issuance.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Some countries tax IMPUTED interest throughout the bond's life, preventing wholesale deferral.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states in general, tax is payable only on capital gains and interest income that have been received. In some countries, an exception to this rule applies to zero-coupon bonds — imputed interest that is taxed throughout a zero-coupon bond's life may be calculated. This method of taxation ensures that tax is paid over the bond's life and that the return on a zero-coupon bond is not taxed entirely as a capital gain.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Taxation at issuance is not the treatment; imputed interest accrues and is taxed over the holding period.

options.A: All countries apply pass-through treatment to mutual fund capital gains, taxing investors on gains realized inside funds.
options.B: No country applies pass-through treatment; fund-level gains are everywhere deferred until the investor sells fund shares.
options.C: the US uses pass-through treatment (realized net capital gains in underlying securities of a fund are treated as if distributed to investors in the year that they arise).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Treatment differs across jurisdictions — pass-through is not universal.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The US uses pass-through: realized net gains inside the fund are treated as distributed to investors.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the US uses pass-through treatment (realized net capital gains in underlying securities of a fund are treated as if distributed to investors in the year that they arise); the UK does not use pass-through treatment (realized gains increase the fund NAV, investors pay taxes when they sell fund shares, deferring capital gains taxes).

Correct. The reading states some countries, such as the US, use pass-through treatment of capital gains in mutual funds. Realized net capital gains in the underlying securities of a fund are treated as if distributed to investors in the year that they arise, and investors need to include the gains on their tax returns. Other countries, such as the UK, do not use pass-through treatment. Realized capital gains within a fund increase the NAV of the fund shares; investors pay taxes on the net capital gain when they sell their fund shares. This treatment defers cap gains tax payments. UK PM decisions on when to realize gains/losses don't affect timing of investor tax payments.

options.A: A callable bond is a Type I liability with both the amount and timing of cash outlays known with certainty.
options.B: Type II liability — known amount but uncertain timing (the call option makes the timing of principal repayment uncertain).
