options.C: A callable bond is a Type IV liability with both the amount and the timing of cash outlays uncertain.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The issuer's call option makes the TIMING of principal repayment uncertain — Type I requires known timing.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Exhibit 15: Type II has known amount but uncertain timing. Examples: callable and putable bonds (the option means principal may be repaid before final maturity); term life insurance policy (timing of death is unknown, but the payout amount is defined). Effective duration is needed for Types II, III, and IV liabilities.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The amount is known; only timing is uncertain — that combination is Type II, not Type IV.

options.A: Type IV liability — both amount and timing uncertain (weather events difficult to predict, making both loss amounts and occurrence timing unknown).
options.B: Property and casualty insurance liabilities are Type II — known amount with uncertain timing.
options.C: Property and casualty insurance liabilities are Type III — uncertain amount with known timing.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Exhibit 15: Type IV has uncertain amount and uncertain timing. Examples: property and casualty insurance (weather events difficult to predict — both loss amounts and timing are uncertain until the event occurs). For Types II, III, and IV liabilities, effective duration is needed, computed using a model for the uncertain cash flows and an initial assumption about the yield curve. Then the yield curve is shifted up and down to obtain new PV estimates.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Weather-driven losses make the AMOUNT uncertain too — Type II requires a known amount.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Timing is also unpredictable — occurrence dates cannot be scheduled — so Type III's known timing fails.

options.A: Residential mortgages with prepayment options are Type I assets, with fully certain cash-flow amounts and timing.
options.B: Type IV assets — the timing of interest and principal cash flows is uncertain due to the prepayment option (a call option) held by the homeowner.
options.C: Residential mortgages with prepayment options are Type III assets, with uncertain amounts but known timing.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The homeowner's prepayment (call) option destroys timing certainty — Type I cannot apply.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Type IV assets — the timing of interest and principal cash flows is uncertain due to the prepayment option (a call option) held by the homeowner; default risk also affects the projected amount of cash flows.

Correct. Per the reading's Modern Mortgage example: residential mortgage loans are Type IV assets to the savings bank. The timing of interest and principal cash flows is uncertain because of the prepayment option held by the homeowner (a complex call option). Homeowners might prepay for many reasons — sale of property, refinance if rates decline. A prepayment model is needed to project timing. Default risk also affects projected amounts. Even if average LTV is 80% (high quality), some loans could have higher LTVs and be more subject to default, especially if home prices decline.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Timing is the UNCERTAIN element; prepayments arrive unpredictably, placing the mortgages in Type IV.

options.A: Liability-based mandates rely on purely tactical active management with no matching of liability payouts.
options.B: Liability-based mandates are implemented exclusively through equity long-short strategies.
options.C: cash flow matching (attempts to ensure all future liability payouts are matched precisely by cash flows from bonds or fixed-income derivatives) and duration matching (based on the duration of assets and liabilities, so both are similarly affected by interest rate changes).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Matching is the core — cash flow matching, duration matching, and contingent immunization are the approaches.
rationales.B: Incorrect. These mandates are fixed-income constructs; equity long-short is not among the approaches.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading identifies within liability-based mandates: (1) cash flow matching — an immunization approach that attempts to ensure all future liability payouts are matched precisely by cash flows from bonds or fixed-income derivatives; (2) duration matching — an immunization approach based on the duration of assets and liabilities (ideally, both the liability portfolio and the bond portfolio should be affected similarly by a change in interest rates); (3) contingent immunization — a hybrid approach that combines immunization with active management when assets exceed the PV of liabilities. These may use derivatives overlays.

options.A: building a dedicated asset portfolio of high-quality fixed-income bonds that, as closely as possible, matches the amount and timing of scheduled cash outflows.
options.B: Cash flow matching means using derivatives to hedge the portfolio's duration risk while ignoring cash flow timing.
options.C: Cash flow matching means continuously buying and selling short-term bills to fund liabilities as they arrive.

rationales.A:
Full statement: building a dedicated asset portfolio of high-quality fixed-income bonds that, as closely as possible, matches the amount and timing of scheduled cash outflows; 'dedicated' means the bonds are placed in a held-to-maturity portfolio.

Correct. The reading defines cash flow matching as a classic strategy to eliminate interest rate risk arising from multiple liabilities by building a dedicated asset portfolio of high-quality fixed-income bonds that, as closely as possible, matches the amount and timing of scheduled cash outflows. 'Dedicated' means the bonds are placed in a held-to-maturity portfolio.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Duration hedging is a different technique; cash flow matching aligns actual bond cash flows with each liability.
rationales.C: Incorrect. A rolling bill program is not the approach — a dedicated portfolio matches amount AND timing of scheduled payouts.

options.A: buy a portfolio of Swedish government bonds working *backwards* from the last liability, matching final maturity + coupon to each obligation and using coupon inflows from longer-maturity bonds to reduce the required par value of shorter-maturity bonds.
options.B: The Simonsson defeasance is accomplished by purchasing a single 5-year zero-coupon bond sized to the total obligation.
options.C: The strategy purchases any available bonds regardless of maturity, since all bonds fund liabilities equally well.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading's Simonsson example, the cash flow matching portfolio is built by starting with the LAST liability and working BACKWARDS. For each liability, the required par value of a bond maturing near that date is computed, subtracting coupons already contributed from later-maturity bonds. E.g., for the Year 5 liability of SEK5.25M, Alfred buys ~SEK4.98M par of the 5.50% May Y5 bond (yields SEK5,253,900 at maturity). Coupons from that bond flow into earlier years, reducing required par values for earlier-maturity bonds. Final holdings: SEK2.96M (2.75% Y2), SEK5.95M (3.50% Y3), SEK3.95M (4.75% Y4), SEK4.98M (5.50% Y5).
rationales.B: Incorrect. One zero maturing in year 5 leaves the year 2-4 payments unfunded; each liability date needs matched cash flow.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Maturity selection is the essence — work BACKWARD from the last liability, matching final maturity plus coupons to each obligation.

options.A: fixed-maturity corporate bond exchange-traded funds (ETFs) with a designated year of maturity and credit risk profile (e.g., 2029 investment-grade corporate bonds).
options.B: A laddered portfolio can be built using only equity ETFs, provided the funds span multiple sectors.
options.C: The only instruments suitable for laddering are individual Treasury bills rolled at each maturity.

rationales.A:
Full statement: fixed-maturity corporate bond exchange-traded funds (ETFs) with a designated year of maturity and credit risk profile (e.g., 2029 investment-grade corporate bonds); ETFs should be more liquid than positions in the actual bonds and provide similar laddered benefits.

Correct. The reading states 'Another way for a wealth manager to build a laddered portfolio for a client is to use fixed-maturity corporate bond exchange-traded funds (ETFs). These ETFs have a designated year of maturity and credit risk profile — for instance, 2024 investment-grade corporate bonds. The passively managed, low-cost ETF seeks to replicate the performance of an index of held-to-maturity investment-grade corporate bonds that mature in that year. Roughly equal positions in 2022 through 2029 fixed-maturity corporate bond ETFs create a laddered portfolio that should provide the same benefits as holding the bonds directly — price stability in the soonest-to-mature ETF and greater convexity than a bullet portfolio. Moreover, the ETFs should be more liquid than positions in the actual bonds.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Equity funds have no maturities to ladder; fixed-maturity BOND ETFs are the vehicle.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Bills alone cluster at the short end; fixed-maturity corporate bond ETFs (e.g., a 2029 investment-grade fund) supply the rungs.

options.A: One limitation of laddered portfolios is that they eliminate all default risk, leaving no credit premium to earn.
options.B: The decision to build a laddered bond portfolio should be weighed against buying shares in a fixed-income mutual fund, especially if the portfolio consists of a limited number of corporate bonds.
options.C: One limitation of laddered portfolios is that their convexity is infinite, making risk management impossible.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Default risk REMAINS in a ladder of corporate bonds — elimination is neither true nor a limitation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: The decision to build a laddered bond portfolio should be weighed against buying shares in a fixed-income mutual fund, especially if the portfolio consists of a limited number of corporate bonds — the mutual fund provides greater diversification of default risk, actual bonds can entail a much higher cost of acquisition, and if the entire investment needs to be liquidated, mutual fund shares can be redeemed more quickly and likely at a better price than selling the bonds.

Correct. The reading states: 'But laddered portfolios are not without limitations. For many investors, the decision to build a laddered bond portfolio should be weighed against buying shares in a fixed-income mutual fund, especially if the portfolio consists of a limited number of corporate bonds. Clearly, the mutual fund provides greater diversification of default risk. Moreover, actual bonds can entail a much higher cost of acquisition. If the entire investment needs to be liquidated, the mutual fund shares can be redeemed more quickly than the bonds can be sold, and likely at a better price.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Ladder convexity is comparatively high but finite; the cited limitation is the comparison against a diversified bond fund, especially for smaller portfolios.

options.A: if a bond has higher positive convexity than an otherwise identical bond, the bond price increases more if rates fall (and decreases less if rates rise) than the duration estimate would suggest.
options.B: Positive convexity is worth nothing to investors because its effects cancel across rising and falling rate environments.
options.C: Higher positive convexity always causes greater losses than an otherwise identical lower-convexity bond.

rationales.A:
Full statement: if a bond has higher positive convexity than an otherwise identical bond, the bond price increases more if rates fall (and decreases less if rates rise) than the duration estimate would suggest; therefore a bond with higher convexity might be expected to have a lower yield-to-maturity than a similar-duration bond with less convexity.

Correct. The reading states, 'All else equal, positive convexity is a valuable feature in bonds. If a bond has higher positive convexity than an otherwise identical bond, then the bond price increases more if interest rates decrease (and decreases less if interest rates increase) than the duration estimate would suggest. This price behavior is valuable to investors; therefore, a bond with higher convexity might be expected to have a lower yield-to-maturity than a similar-duration bond with less convexity.' Convexity is affected by cash flow dispersion — higher dispersion → higher convexity.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The effects reinforce rather than cancel — gaining more when rates fall AND losing less when rates rise is valuable asymmetry.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Convexity cushions losses; the higher-convexity bond loses LESS when rates rise.

options.A: Higher cash flow dispersion produces lower convexity, which is why barbells carry less convexity than bullets.
options.B: higher cash flow dispersion → higher convexity, so barbell portfolios (with widely dispersed cash flows) have more convexity than bullet portfolios (with concentrated cash flows) for the same duration.
options.C: Cash flow dispersion has no impact on convexity; the two properties are independent.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The relationship runs the other way — dispersion RAISES convexity, and barbells out-convex bullets.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states convexity is affected by the dispersion of cash flows — the variance of the times to receipt of cash flow. Higher cash flow dispersion leads to an increase in convexity. This is in contrast to Macaulay duration, which measures the weighted average of the times to cash flow receipt. All else equal, bonds with longer durations also have higher convexity. This is why the barbell portfolio in Example 1 has 40.229 convexity vs 26.5 for the bullet with the same duration.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Dispersion directly drives convexity; spreading cash flows around the duration point is what creates it.

options.A: The three primary yield curve risk factors from principal components analysis are all forms of issuer-specific credit risk.
options.B: level (parallel shift ~82% of yield curve changes), slope (twist/flattening or steepening ~12%), and curvature/shape (butterfly movement ~4%).
options.C: PCA identifies a single yield curve risk factor: central bank policy risk, explaining virtually all curve movement.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Credit spread risk is a separate factor family; the PCA factors are level, slope, and curvature.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states primary yield curve risk factors are categorized by three types: (1) change in level (parallel shift), (2) change in slope (flattening or steepening twist), and (3) change in shape or curvature (butterfly movement). Principal components analysis was used to decompose yield curve changes: level, slope, and curvature movements over time accounted for approximately 82%, 12%, and 4%, respectively, of US Treasury yield curve changes. The consistency of these results over time and across global markets underscores their importance in active yield curve strategies.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The decomposition yields THREE factors — level (~82%), slope (~12%), and curvature (~4%) — not a single policy factor.

options.A: Yield curve slope is measured as the average yield across all maturities on the curve.
options.B: Yield curve slope is measured as the standard deviation of yields across the maturity spectrum.
options.C: the difference in basis points between the yield-to-maturity on a long-maturity bond and the yield-to-maturity on a shorter-maturity bond (e.g., the 2s30s spread of 127 bps in July 2020: 30-year at 1.43% minus 2-year at 0.16%).

rationales.A: Incorrect. An average describes the curve's LEVEL; slope is the long-minus-short yield difference.
rationales.B: Incorrect. A standard deviation describes yield dispersion, not the directional difference between long and short maturities.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the difference in basis points between the yield-to-maturity on a long-maturity bond and the yield-to-maturity on a shorter-maturity bond (e.g., the 2s30s spread of 127 bps in July 2020: 30-year at 1.43% minus 2-year at 0.16%); widening spread is a steepening, narrowing is a flattening, and negative spread means an inverted curve.

Correct. The reading defines yield curve slope as the difference in basis points between the yield-to-maturity on a long-maturity bond and a shorter-maturity bond. Example: as of July 2020, the slope measured by the 2s30s spread — the difference between the 30-year Treasury bond (1.43%) and the 2-year Treasury note (0.16%) yields-to-maturity — was 127 bps. As the spread widens the curve steepens; as it narrows it flattens; if the spread turns negative (as just before the 2008 GFC), the yield curve is inverted.

options.A: Butterfly spread = −(Short-term yield) + (2 × Medium-term yield) − Long-term yield — positive spread indicates a 'humped' or concave shape at the midpoint; negative spread indicates a 'saucer' or convex shape.
options.B: The butterfly spread is computed as short-term yield minus twice the medium-term yield plus the long-term yield.
options.C: The butterfly spread equals the long-term yield minus the short-term yield.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Equation 2, Butterfly spread = −(Short-term yield) + (2 × Medium-term yield) − Long-term yield. The butterfly spread takes on larger positive values when the yield curve has more curvature. Positive butterfly spread indicates a 'humped' or concave shape to the midpoint of the curve; negative butterfly spread indicates a 'saucer' or convex shape. It changes when intermediate-term yield changes are of a different magnitude than those on the wings (short and long ends). Exhibit 3 uses 2y, 10y, and 30y tenors.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The signs are reversed — the formula is minus short, PLUS twice medium, minus long.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Long minus short is the SLOPE; the butterfly measures curvature with the 2× medium-term center.

options.A: Rolling down the yield curve means selling all bonds immediately at the initial purchase date to capture the entry spread.
options.B: buying bonds with maturity beyond the investment horizon and selling them at the end at a higher price (lower YTM due to the upward-sloping curve as the bond has aged).
options.C: Rolling down the yield curve means continuously shifting the portfolio into shorter maturities as time passes.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The strategy HOLDS the bond, capturing the price appreciation from riding down the upward-sloping curve.
rationales.B:
Full statement: buying bonds with maturity beyond the investment horizon and selling them at the end at a higher price (lower YTM due to the upward-sloping curve as the bond has aged); with an upward-sloping curve, this offers a total return (higher coupon plus price appreciation) greater than buying a bond with maturity matching the horizon.

Correct. The reading states that 'rolling down' the yield curve incorporates coupon income (adjusted over time for any price difference from par) but also additional return from time passage and the investor's ability to sell the shorter-maturity bond in the future at a higher price (lower YTM due to the upward-sloping curve). If the yield curve is upward-sloping, buying bonds with maturity beyond the investment horizon offers a total return (higher coupon plus price appreciation) greater than the purchase of a bond with maturity matching the investment horizon if the curve remains static.
rationales.C: Incorrect. No shifting is required; the bond's own aging lowers its YTM (on an upward-sloping curve) and lifts its price.

options.A: The repo carry trade involves borrowing at long-term rates and lending at short-term rates.
options.B: The repo carry trade involves trading cash for stocks to capture the equity risk premium.
options.C: buying a long-term security and financing it at a short-term rate below the long-term YTM — that is, earning a positive 'repo carry'.

rationales.A: Incorrect. That reverses the direction — the trade FUNDS long-term bond positions at LOWER short-term repo rates.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The trade is a fixed-income financing structure, not an equity strategy.
rationales.C:
Full statement: buying a long-term security and financing it at a short-term rate below the long-term YTM — that is, earning a positive 'repo carry'; at the end of the trade the bond is sold and the repo is unwound.

Correct. The reading defines the repo carry trade as buying a long-term security and financing it at a short-term rate below the long-term YTM — earning a positive 'repo carry'. At the end of the trade, the bond is sold and the repo is unwound. This uses the repo market for leverage: cash-based strategy but with leverage risk added.

options.A: Long futures position (purchase contract for future bond delivery) and receive-fixed swap (fixed-rate receiver on an interest rate swap) — both synthetically increase duration.
options.B: Exhibit 7's derivatives-based static strategies consist of cash bond purchases only, with no derivative instruments.
options.C: Exhibit 7's strategies are short-only positions designed to reduce portfolio duration.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Long futures position (purchase contract for future bond delivery) and receive-fixed swap (fixed-rate receiver on an interest rate swap) — both synthetically increase duration; long futures relies on price appreciation, receive-fixed generates 'swap carry' (receive fixed swap rate, pay MRR).

Correct. Per Exhibit 7, derivatives-based static YC strategies to synthetically increase duration are: (1) long futures position — purchase contract for future bond delivery; targeted return = (ΔPrice / ΔBond yield) − Margin cost; (2) receive-fixed swap — fixed-rate receiver on interest rate swap; targeted return = (Swap rate − MRR) + (ΔSwap MTM / ΔSwap yield). Similar to receive-fixed swap being like the cash-based repo carry but with 'swap carry' component.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The exhibit's subject is DERIVATIVES — long futures and receive-fixed swaps.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Both listed positions ADD duration synthetically; they are duration-extending, not duration-reducing.

options.A: Derivatives require full cash payment upfront equal to the notional value, which is the source of their leverage.
options.B: the initial cash outlay for a derivative is limited to initial margin or collateral — as opposed to the full price for a cash bond purchase — so derivatives have a high degree of implicit leverage.
options.C: Derivatives cannot be leveraged because clearinghouses require one-for-one collateralization.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Full upfront payment is the OPPOSITE of the leverage mechanism — only margin or collateral is posted.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the initial cash outlay for a derivative is limited to initial margin or collateral — as opposed to the full price for a cash bond purchase — so derivatives have a high degree of implicit leverage; a small move in price/yield can have a very large effect on a derivative's MTM value relative to the margin posted.

Correct. The reading states, 'since the initial cash outlay for a derivative is limited to initial margin or collateral as opposed to the full price for a cash bond purchase, derivatives have a high degree of implicit leverage. A small move in price/yield can have a very large effect on a derivative's mark-to-market value (MTM) relative to the margin posted. This outsized price effect makes derivatives effective instruments for fixed-income portfolio management.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Margin-based exposure IS the implicit leverage; collateral is a fraction of notional.

options.A: The butterfly strategy for curvature uses bullet-only exposure concentrated at a single maturity point.
options.B: combining a long bullet with a short barbell portfolio (or vice versa) — short-term and long-term bond positions of the barbell form the 'wings', while the intermediate-term bullet forms the 'body'.
options.C: The butterfly strategy purchases all maturities in equal amounts across the curve.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A lone bullet is one wing of the trade; the butterfly PAIRS a bullet body against barbell wings.
rationales.B:
Full statement: combining a long bullet with a short barbell portfolio (or vice versa) — short-term and long-term bond positions of the barbell form the 'wings', while the intermediate-term bullet forms the 'body'; the investor is either long or short both a short-term and long-term bond and enters an intermediate-term bullet in the opposite direction.

Correct. Per the reading, 'The most common yield curve curvature strategy combines a long bullet with a short barbell portfolio (or vice versa) in what is referred to as a butterfly strategy to capitalize on expected yield curve shape changes.' The short-term and long-term bond positions of the barbell form the 'wings', while the intermediate-term bullet forms the 'body' of the butterfly. Unlike steepener/flatteners, the investor is either 'long' or 'short' both a short-term and long-term bond and enters into an intermediate-term bullet trade in the opposite direction.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Equal weighting across maturities is a ladder, not a butterfly; the butterfly is long bullet/short barbell or the reverse.

options.A: combining a 'long' shorter-dated bond position with a 'short' longer-dated bond position — this generates a gain from an increase in yield curve slope (long shorter maturity gains as short-end rates decrease or shorter rates fall less than longer rates rise).
options.B: A duration-neutral steepener is achieved by purchasing longer-dated bonds only, concentrating exposure at the long end.
options.C: A duration-neutral steepener is achieved by buying both short- and long-term bonds in market-value-matched amounts.

rationales.A:
Full statement: combining a 'long' shorter-dated bond position with a 'short' longer-dated bond position — this generates a gain from an increase in yield curve slope (long shorter maturity gains as short-end rates decrease or shorter rates fall less than longer rates rise); downside risk is yield curve flattening.

Correct. Per the reading, yield curve steepener strategies seek to gain from an increase in yield curve slope (or a greater difference between long-term and short-term YTM). This may be achieved by combining a 'long' shorter-dated bond position with a 'short' longer-dated bond position. Example: buying the 2-year Treasury and selling the 10-year Treasury at par with position weights that give zero net portfolio duration; portfolio gains if slope increases (whether from decline in 2-year yield or rise in 10-year yield). Downside risk is yield curve flattening.
rationales.B: Incorrect. A long-end bullet has large net duration and loses as the curve steepens — the opposite structure.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Long both wings is a barbell, not a steepener; the steepener pairs LONG short-dated against SHORT long-dated positions at net zero duration.

options.A: A bear steepener is a fall in long-term yields greater than the fall in short-term yields.
options.B: A bear steepener describes a stable-yield environment in which the curve's slope drifts without direction.
options.C: a rise in long-term yields more than short-term yields — could result from a jump in long-term rates amid higher growth and inflation expectations while short-term rates remain unchanged.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Falling yields are BULL moves; that pattern is a bull steepener only if short rates fall faster.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The bear steepener is a directional RISE — long-term yields up more than short-term, often on growth and inflation expectations.
rationales.C:
Full statement: a rise in long-term yields more than short-term yields — could result from a jump in long-term rates amid higher growth and inflation expectations while short-term rates remain unchanged; an analyst might expect the next central bank policy change to be tightening.

Correct. The reading describes bear steepening: long-term YTM rise more than short-term YTM. This could result from a jump in long-term rates amid higher growth and inflation expectations while short-term rates remain unchanged. In this case, an analyst might expect the next central bank policy change to be a monetary tightening to curb inflation.

options.A: reverse the exposure profile of a steepener — namely, a 'short' short-term bond position and a 'long' long-term bond position.
options.B: Yield curve flattener strategies are structured to gain from an increase in the curve's slope.
options.C: Flattener strategies are unaffected by changes in the slope of the yield curve.

rationales.A:
Full statement: reverse the exposure profile of a steepener — namely, a 'short' short-term bond position and a 'long' long-term bond position; gain from an anticipated narrowing of the difference between long-term and short-term yields.

Correct. The reading states flattener strategies may use a barbell that reverses the exposure profile of a steepener — namely, a 'short' short-term bond position and a 'long' long-term bond position. Yield curve flattening involves an anticipated narrowing of the difference between long-term and short-term YTM. Two variations: bear flattening (short-term rates rise) and bull flattening (long-term rates fall — often flight-to-quality). Flatteners may be duration-neutral, net long duration (bull flattener), or net short duration (bear flattener).
rationales.B: Incorrect. Gaining from slope INCREASES is the steepener; the flattener profits when slope decreases.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Slope sensitivity is the trade's entire purpose — short the short end, long the long end.

options.A: increases portfolio duration — adds convexity to the portfolio and is exercised if the bond price appreciates beyond the strike (i.e., generally at a lower YTM).
options.B: A purchased bond call option decreases portfolio duration, offsetting long cash bond exposure.
options.C: A purchased bond call option has no duration impact because option positions carry only volatility exposure.

rationales.A:
Full statement: increases portfolio duration — adds convexity to the portfolio and is exercised if the bond price appreciates beyond the strike (i.e., generally at a lower YTM); payoff = Max(Bond price at lower yield − Strike, 0) − Call premium.

Correct. Per Exhibit 24, a long bond call option is the purchase of the right to take forward bond delivery. Targeted return = Max(Bond price at lower yield − Strike, 0) − Call premium. Portfolio duration impact: increase portfolio duration (long bond position exposure via option). A call option adds convexity to the portfolio and is exercised if the bond price appreciates beyond the strike (at lower YTM).
rationales.B: Incorrect. Duration reduction comes from PUT purchases; the call gains as bond prices rise, adding duration.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The call has positive delta to bond prices — a genuine duration (and convexity) contribution.

options.A: a negative KRD contribution — for example, the short 5-year active position has KRD of −0.857 = 4.902 × (−46/263).
options.B: A short position in a maturity bucket produces a positive key rate duration contribution.
options.C: Short positions produce no key rate duration contribution because KRD is defined only for long holdings.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading's active portfolio example: the short 5-year active position has a negative key rate duration of −0.857 = 4.902 × (−46/263). Negative position values (representing 'short' positions) produce negative KRD contributions. This allows active managers to explicitly express short views on specific parts of the yield curve.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Positive contributions come from LONG positions; the short's sign flips negative.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Shorts contribute with negative sign — e.g., the short 5-year position's KRD of −0.857 = 4.902 × (−46/263).

options.A: R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1, for a single asset — combining foreign-currency return with FX return.
options.B: The domestic-currency return on a foreign bond is the product of the two component returns: R_DC = R_FC × R_FX.
options.C: The domestic-currency return is the simple sum of the foreign-currency return and the FX return: R_DC = R_FC + R_FX.

rationales.A:
Full statement: R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1, for a single asset — combining foreign-currency return with FX return; portfolio version uses weighted sums.

Correct. Per Equations 12-13: For a single asset, R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1. For a portfolio, R_DC = Σ ω_i (1 + R_FC,i)(1 + R_FX,i) − 1. R_DC and R_FC are domestic and foreign currency returns; R_FX is percentage change of domestic vs foreign currency; ω_i are portfolio weights summing to 1. This combines the third factor (E(ΔPrice due to view of benchmark yield)) and fifth factor (E(ΔPrice due to view of currency value changes)) from Equation 1.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Multiplying the returns is not the compounding relationship.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The simple sum drops the cross-term; the returns compound as (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1.

options.A: Covered interest rate parity establishes no relationship between spot and forward exchange rates.
options.B: F(DC/FC, T) = S_0(DC/FC) × [(1 + r_DC)^T / (1 + r_FC)^T], where F is forward rate, S is spot, r_DC and r_FC are domestic and foreign risk-free rates.
options.C: Covered interest rate parity states that the forward rate equals the spot rate in all circumstances.

rationales.A: Incorrect. CIP defines the relationship precisely through the interest rate differential.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Equation 14: F(DC/FC, T) = S_0(DC/FC) × [(1 + r_DC)^T / (1 + r_FC)^T]. If an investor uses a forward contract to fully hedge foreign currency cash flows, she should expect to earn the domestic risk-free rate. This implies a higher-yielding currency trades at a forward discount, while a lower-yielding currency trades at a premium. In the reading's example: 1.1218 spot vs 1.187 2-year forward, consistent with USD > EUR rates in 2019.
rationales.C: Incorrect. F = S only when the two risk-free rates are equal; generally F = S × (1 + r_DC)^T / (1 + r_FC)^T.

options.A: The MXN carry trade fell short of expectations because Mexican rates fell dramatically over the horizon.
options.B: The shortfall arose because the USD funding rate changed dramatically during the trade.
options.C: over the horizon, 90-day MXN yields rose (from 7.70% to 8.20% at 270 days) and MXN depreciated against USD by year-end (from 19.15 to 19.65).

rationales.A: Incorrect. MXN 90-day yields ROSE (7.70% to 8.20% by day 270), hurting the reinvestment leg — they did not fall.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The USD borrowing cost was FIXED on the one-year loan; the drag came from rising MXN yields and peso depreciation (19.15 to 19.65).
rationales.C:
Full statement: over the horizon, 90-day MXN yields rose (from 7.70% to 8.20% at 270 days) and MXN depreciated against USD by year-end (from 19.15 to 19.65); late-period MXN depreciation undercut trade profitability despite higher MXN yields.

Correct. Per Example 12: 'Note that 90-day MXN YTMs rose and that MXN depreciated slightly versus USD over the 360-day period. If the manager had rolled over this trade for the full 12 months, the realized return would have been ~3.61%. While the cross-currency carry trade was ultimately profitable, it was exposed to risks over the horizon; moreover, despite the rise in 90-day MXN YTMs, a late-period MXN depreciation undercut the profitability of the trade.' Carry trades are unhedged and most successful in stable (low volatility) markets.

options.A: The cross-currency basis is always zero under all market conditions, as arbitrage instantly closes any gap.
options.B: the difference between the direct USD interest rate and the synthetic USD interest rate derived from swapping JPY into US dollars — a positive (negative) basis means the direct rate is higher (lower) than the synthetic rate.
options.C: The cross-currency basis is simply the difference between EUR and USD policy rates.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Persistent non-zero basis is well documented — post-crisis funding frictions keep arbitrage from closing it.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the difference between the direct USD interest rate and the synthetic USD interest rate derived from swapping JPY into US dollars — a positive (negative) basis means the direct rate is higher (lower) than the synthetic rate; covered interest rate parity suggests it should be close to zero, but Exhibit 29 shows persistent negative basis for JPY and EUR post-2008 GFC.

Correct. Per the reading, 'The "basis" or spread, as shown in the cross-currency basis swap, is the difference between the USD interest rate and the synthetic USD interest rate derived from swapping JPY into US dollars. A positive (negative) currency basis means that the direct USD interest rate is higher (lower) than the synthetic USD interest rate. While covered interest rate parity suggests that cross-currency basis should be close to zero, Exhibit 29 shows that the JPY and EUR cross-currency basis was persistently negative following the 2008 global financial crisis.' Du, Tepper, and Verdelhan (2018) attribute this to higher post-2008 financial intermediation costs and international imbalances.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The basis is the gap between DIRECT USD funding and SYNTHETIC USD funding created by swapping (e.g., JPY) into dollars.

options.A: Portfolio risk and return impact analysis is best illustrated using duration alone, which captures all relevant yield curve risk.
options.B: a pair of examples (like Examples 14 and 15) that integrate rolldown vs carry, changes in level/slope/shape of a single-currency yield curve, and extension to multiple currencies with spot and forward FX rates related to relative interest rates.
options.C: The analysis is best conducted through purely qualitative discussion without numerical scenarios.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Duration alone misses slope and shape effects — the integrated examples exist to capture them.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the reading, 'The fixed-income portfolio risk and return impact of rolldown return versus carry, changes in the level, slope, and shape of a single currency yield curve, and an extension to multiple currencies (where spot and forward FX rates are related to relative interest rates) are best illustrated with a pair of examples.' Examples 14 (single-currency Treasury scenarios) and 15 (AUD bullet vs barbell) integrate all key elements.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Quantitative worked examples (rolldown vs carry, level/slope/shape changes, FX) are the reading's chosen vehicle.

options.A: Successful active yield curve managers focus exclusively on parallel level shifts of the curve, since level movements explain most yield variance and render slope and shape views an unnecessary complication.
options.B: Successful active yield curve managers avoid all derivative instruments, implementing their curve views exclusively through cash bond purchases and sales to eliminate counterparty and margin complexity.
options.C: integrate views on level, slope, and shape of the yield curve using appropriate strategies (cash and derivatives), account for convexity effects when relevant, use KRDs to identify shaping risk, evaluate cross-currency exposures via CIP and cross-currency swaps, and use scenario analysis to stress-test against potential adverse outcomes.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Slope and curvature views are integral — level-only focus leaves most of the toolkit unused.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Futures, swaps, and options are central instruments; avoiding them forfeits efficient implementation.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading's overall framework: successful active YC management integrates: (1) yield level view driving bullet/barbell/synthetic duration adjustments; (2) slope view driving steepener/flattener strategies; (3) shape view driving butterfly strategies; (4) volatility view driving option/embedded-option choices; (5) KRD analysis to detect shaping risk between portfolio and benchmark; (6) cross-currency strategies where uncovered interest parity divergence provides carry opportunities but exchange rate volatility can undermine profitability; (7) scenario analysis to quantify tail risks. This holistic framework is essential.

options.A: $1,945,628 (3.891%) — larger than Portfolio A's $1,236,563 due to Portfolio B's higher 10y exposure (which sees the 100 bp yield decline).
options.B: Under the bull flattening scenario, Portfolio B gains $1,236,563 (2.473%), less than Portfolio A.
options.C: Portfolio B gains nothing under the bull flattening because barbell structures are immune to curve changes.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Example 14: under bull flattening (5y −50 bp, 10y −100 bp), Portfolio A gains 2.473% ($1,236,563) while Portfolio B gains 3.891% ($1,945,628). Portfolio B's larger gain reflects its 41.06% weight in the 10-year Treasury which experienced the 100 bp yield decline. This demonstrates that duration-matched portfolios perform differently under slope scenarios.
rationales.B: Incorrect. $1,236,563 is PORTFOLIO A's gain; B's larger 10-year exposure captures the 100 bp long-end rally.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The barbell is highly exposed to the long end — it gains the MOST from the 10-year yield decline.

options.A: The AUD bullet and barbell trades are identical in outcome, making the evaluation moot.
options.B: The manager's evaluation depends only on the FX view, since AUD appreciation dominates any rate effect.
options.C: portfolio value change combines rolldown return (bullet 99.75 vs barbell 100.00 expected end-of-horizon price under stable curve), duration effect (60 bp × 3.95), plus FX return from 0.25% AUD appreciation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The structures respond differently to the parallel shift through their differing duration, convexity, and rolldown profiles.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Both legs matter — rolldown and rate effects on portfolio value combine with the 0.25% AUD appreciation in the total return.
rationales.C:
Full statement: portfolio value change combines rolldown return (bullet 99.75 vs barbell 100.00 expected end-of-horizon price under stable curve), duration effect (60 bp × 3.95), plus FX return from 0.25% AUD appreciation — expected total AUD return then converted using R_DC = (1+R_FC)(1+R_FX) − 1.

Correct. Per the AUD Example 15 framework, evaluating cross-currency yield curve strategy return requires combining: (1) rolldown return (bullet 99.75 vs barbell 100.00 in 1 year under stable curve = different rolldown magnitudes), (2) duration-based response to 60 bp parallel decline (60 bp × 3.95 duration ≈ 2.37% price gain), (3) any convexity contribution, and (4) FX return of 0.25%. Total return per Equation 12: R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1. This scenario illustrates how the reading's framework integrates all elements of Equation 1.

options.A: The integrated framework handles only single-currency exposures, leaving foreign bonds outside its scope.
options.B: quantifies how unexpected changes to level/slope/shape of the yield curve as well as currency changes can impact portfolio value.
options.C: The integrated framework ignores convexity, treating price responses as linear in yield changes.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The cross-currency return equations (12-13) extend the framework to foreign-currency positions.
rationales.B:
Full statement: quantifies how unexpected changes to level/slope/shape of the yield curve as well as currency changes can impact portfolio value — Equation 3 for single-currency and Equation 13 for multi-currency portfolios.

Correct. Per the reading, 'The factors affecting the expected return of a fixed-income portfolio were summarized in Equation 1. The key underlying assumption in this calculation is that the inputs rely on the fixed-income manager's expectations under an active strategy. As we have seen earlier, unexpected changes to the level, slope, and shape of the yield curve as well as currency changes can impact a portfolio's value in a number of ways — as quantified by the use of portfolio duration and convexity statistics in Equation 3 for a single currency and in Equation 13 for a multi-currency portfolio.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Convexity enters through Equation 3 — second-order price effects are part of the quantification.

options.A: Expected loss is computed as the probability of default alone: EL = POD.
options.B: EL = LGD × POD, where LGD = EE × (1 − RR) and POD is the probability of default; CVA is Σ(PV of Expected Loss).
options.C: Expected loss is computed as the loss given default alone: EL = LGD.

rationales.A: Incorrect. POD alone carries no dollar magnitude; it must be weighted by the loss severity LGD.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Exhibit 2 CVA framework: Expected Exposure (EE) × (1 − Recovery Rate) = LGD (Loss Given Default). LGD × POD (Probability of Default) = EL (Expected Loss). PV of EL discounted at risk-free rate, summed = CVA. This provides the PV of credit risk for a loan, bond, or derivative.
rationales.C: Incorrect. LGD alone ignores likelihood; the probability weight POD completes EL = LGD × POD, with CVA the sum of PV of expected losses.

options.A: The asset swap spread is identical to the Z-spread for every bond, differing only in name.
options.B: the difference between the bond's fixed coupon rate and the fixed rate on an interest rate swap versus MRR — matches the coupon dates for the remaining life of the bond.
options.C: The asset swap spread is the bond issuer's CDS spread quoted in the derivatives market.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The Z-spread is measured over the zero (spot) curve; the ASW references the swap fixed rate versus MRR against the bond's coupon.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the difference between the bond's fixed coupon rate and the fixed rate on an interest rate swap versus MRR — matches the coupon dates for the remaining life of the bond; if bond priced at par, this spread approximately equals the credit risk over MRR.

Correct. Per the reading, ASW is the difference between the bond's fixed coupon rate and the fixed rate on an interest rate swap versus MRR that matches the coupon dates for the remaining life of the bond. If purchased at par, the asset swap transforms the fixed-rate coupon to an equivalent spread over MRR for the life of the bond. Example 5: bank bond coupon 2.75% vs 8y swap rate 1.647% → ASW = 110.3 bps. Under bond default, the asset manager still faces mark-to-market settlement of the swap.
rationales.C: Incorrect. CDS spreads come from the credit derivatives market; the ASW is a cash-bond-versus-swap construct.

options.A: The Z-spread is identical to the simple yield spread over a single benchmark bond.
options.B: The Z-spread measures the bond's spread attributable to interest rate volatility over the zero curve.
options.C: a constant spread over a government (or interest rate swap) spot curve added to each spot rate such that PV of bond cash flows equals the bond price — accurately captures term structure of zero rates.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The yield spread uses one benchmark point; the Z-spread is a constant addition to EVERY spot rate on the curve.
rationales.B: Incorrect. 'Zero-volatility' means volatility is assumed away — the same constant spread across the curve equates PV to price.
rationales.C:
Full statement: a constant spread over a government (or interest rate swap) spot curve added to each spot rate such that PV of bond cash flows equals the bond price — accurately captures term structure of zero rates; more complex than G-spread/I-spread but limited to option-free bonds.

Correct. Per Equation 2 and the reading, the Z-spread is a constant spread over a government (or swap) spot curve such that PV = Σ [PMT/(1 + z_i + Z)^i + FV/(1 + z_N + Z)^N]. Where the bond price is a function of cash flows in the numerator with respective benchmark spot rates z_1...z_N derived from the swap or government yield curve and a constant Z-spread per period. More accurate than G-spread or I-spread but more complex; limited to option-free bonds.

options.A: a generalization of the Z-spread calculation that incorporates bond option pricing based on assumed interest rate volatility.
options.B: The option-adjusted spread is identical to the Z-spread for all bonds, whether or not options are embedded.
options.C: The option-adjusted spread is defined only for callable bonds and cannot be computed for other structures.

rationales.A:
Full statement: a generalization of the Z-spread calculation that incorporates bond option pricing based on assumed interest rate volatility — the constant yield spread over the zero curve which makes the arbitrage-free value of a bond with embedded options equal to its market price.

Correct. Per the reading, the OAS is a generalization of the Z-spread calculation that incorporates bond option pricing based on assumed interest rate volatility. The OAS is the constant yield spread over the zero curve which makes the arbitrage-free value of such a bond equal to its market price. Note that the Z-spread for an option-free bond is simply its OAS, assuming zero volatility. OAS provides a consistent basis for comparing credit risk yield spreads for option-free, callable, putable, and structured fixed-income instruments.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Equality holds ONLY for option-free bonds; embedded options drive the two apart.
rationales.C: Incorrect. OAS generalizes across option-free, callable, putable, and structured instruments — that generality is its point.

options.A: OAS is considered most appropriate because it is the simplest spread measure to compute.
options.B: it provides a consistent basis for comparing credit risk yield spreads for option-free, callable, putable, and structured fixed-income instruments.
options.C: OAS is considered most appropriate because it ignores interest rate volatility entirely.

rationales.A: Incorrect. OAS is among the most COMPLEX measures — its value is consistency, not simplicity.
rationales.B:
Full statement: it provides a consistent basis for comparing credit risk yield spreads for option-free, callable, putable, and structured fixed-income instruments — despite its main drawbacks (highly dependent on volatility and other model assumptions).

Correct. Per the reading, 'The OAS approach is the most appropriate yield spread measure for active fixed-income portfolio managers because it provides a consistent basis for comparing credit risk yield spreads for option-free, callable, putable, and structured fixed-income instruments.' OAS calculations rely on models incorporating current term structure, interest rate volatility, term structure model factors, and specific option-based features. Main drawback: highly dependent on volatility and other model assumptions. For structured instruments, returns are highly dependent on prepayment vs extension risk.
rationales.C: Incorrect. OAS INCORPORATES assumed volatility through option pricing; ignoring volatility is the Z-spread's limitation.

options.A: divides the credit universe into sectors of comparable companies, then evaluates each issuer's implied credit risk by comparing company-specific financial information to spread-related compensation for assuming default, credit migration, and liquidity risks.
options.B: A bottom-up credit approach focuses only on macroeconomic factors such as growth, inflation, and policy rates.
options.C: A bottom-up credit approach ignores individual bond issuers entirely, allocating by sector aggregates alone.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading states, 'Once the credit universe has been divided into sectors and prospective bonds identified, the investor evaluates each issuer's implied credit risk comparing company-specific financial information to spread-related compensation for assuming default, credit migration, and liquidity risks for comparative purposes. Beyond the prospects within a company's industry, its competitive position within that industry, and operating history, financial ratios are a valuable tool to compare creditworthiness across firms.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Macro-first analysis is the TOP-DOWN approach.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Issuer-level comparison within sectors of comparable companies is the essence of bottom-up.

options.A: Reduced form credit models use market-based variables only, inferring default from equity prices and asset volatility.
options.B: Reduced form models are based purely on qualitative analyst judgment without quantitative inputs.
options.C: solve for default intensity (POD over a specific time period) using observable company-specific variables such as financial ratios and recovery assumptions as well as macroeconomic variables (economic growth, market volatility measures).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Equity-market-based asset-value inference describes STRUCTURAL models.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reduced form models are quantitative — solving for default intensity from observable company variables such as financial ratios.
rationales.C:
Full statement: solve for default intensity (POD over a specific time period) using observable company-specific variables such as financial ratios and recovery assumptions as well as macroeconomic variables (economic growth, market volatility measures) — Altman's Z-score is an early example.

Correct. Per the reading, reduced form models solve for default intensity, or the POD over a specific time period, using observable company-specific variables such as financial ratios and recovery assumptions as well as macroeconomic variables, including economic growth and market volatility measures. Altman's (1968) Z-score combined liquidity (working capital/total assets), profitability (retained earnings/total assets), asset efficiency (EBIT/total assets), market vs book equity, and asset turnover (sales/total assets) into a composite score to classify manufacturing firms as likely to remain solvent or go bankrupt.

options.A: An increase in the issuer's stock price volatility would decrease the structural model's probability of default.
options.B: increase the POD, because higher equity volatility increases the likelihood that the market value of the issuer's assets will fall below the default threshold.
options.C: Stock price volatility has no effect on the probability of default in a structural model.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Higher volatility widens the distribution of asset values, RAISING the chance assets fall below the default threshold.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 15: 'Higher equity volatility increases the likelihood that the market value of the issuer's assets will fall below the default threshold.' Structural models use asset volatility to determine the likelihood of reaching the default point. In contrast, a higher coverage ratio (higher cash flow) implies improved ability to service debt (lower POD), and a decrease in leverage ratio (less debt vs equity) reduces likelihood of financial distress.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Equity volatility is a critical structural-model input — it drives the asset-value distribution.

options.A: Active credit investors incorporate ESG through purely random issuer selection weighted by market values.
options.B: Active credit investors ignore ESG entirely, treating it as immaterial to credit outcomes.
options.C: screens (exclude industries or issuers below ESG threshold), targeting relatively favorable ESG issuers within a given sector/rating while matching index risk/return, and directly funding ESG-specific initiatives.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Random selection is not a strategy; the three ways are screens, best-in-class targeting, and thematic/impact approaches.
rationales.B: Incorrect. ESG adoption in credit is growing and structured — exclusionary screens, favorable-issuer tilts, and dedicated mandates.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading: 'Active credit investors usually incorporate ESG factors into portfolio strategies in one of three basic ways: (1) The use of screens to either exclude specific industries with less favorable ESG characteristics (firearms, tobacco, coal), or to rule out specific companies or sovereign issuers with ESG-specific ratings below a threshold; (2) Use of ESG ratings to target issuers within a given sector or rating category with relatively favorable ESG characteristics while matching a specific index risk and return; (3) Targeting fixed-income investments that directly fund ESG-specific initiatives.' ESG and credit ratings tend to be positively correlated.

options.A: In an economic downturn, high-yield spread curves steepen dramatically as long-dated risk dominates.
options.B: flatten or invert — because the probability of downgrade or default is higher in the near term than the long term, HY often experience more pronounced flattening/inversion than investment-grade.
options.C: High-yield spread curves remain unchanged across the economic cycle.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Dramatic steepening characterizes recovery/expansion phases; downturns concentrate default risk in the NEAR term.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 2 solution: 'High-yield issuers often experience more pronounced flattening or credit spread curve inversion in an economic downturn because the probability of downgrade or default is higher in the near term than the long term.' While investment-grade and high-yield issuers both experience declining profitability, this usually leads to a flatter credit spread curve for investment-grade issuers and often to credit spread curve inversion for high-yield issuers.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The cycle reshapes credit curves — downturns flatten or invert HY curves as near-term default probability jumps.

options.A: OTC markets — liquidity rests with individual dealers, their specific portfolio and depth of inventory, and appetite to supply liquidity at a cost.
options.B: Corporate bond market liquidity is characterized by highly liquid centralized exchange trading like equities.
options.C: Corporate bond markets have no liquidity at all, making secondary trading impossible.

rationales.A:
Full statement: OTC markets — liquidity rests with individual dealers, their specific portfolio and depth of inventory, and appetite to supply liquidity at a cost; higher regulatory capital requirements post-2008 reduced bond inventories, driving growth of electronic trading platforms.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Bonds primarily trade in over-the-counter (OTC) markets as opposed to on an exchange. OTC market liquidity rests with individual dealers, their specific portfolio and depth of inventory, and appetite to supply liquidity at a cost. Corporate bonds are traditionally traded on a request-for-quote basis. The use of electronic trading platforms for bond trading has grown because higher regulatory capital requirements reduced bond inventories among dealers after the 2008-09 global financial crisis. While electronic trading platforms comprised less than one-third of individual corporate bond trading volume as of 2020, trading in bond portfolios and bond ETFs has grown in importance.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Bond markets are OTC-dominated; liquidity rests with individual dealers' inventories and risk appetite.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Liquidity exists but is dealer-intermediated and uneven across issues.

options.A: TRACE is a US equity exchange where corporate bonds are listed for order-book trading.
options.B: a separate ex-post liquidity gauge specific to the US corporate bond market — the Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine reporting system introduced in 2002 to track real-time price and volume reporting for bond transactions.
options.C: TRACE is an exchange-traded fund tracking the US corporate bond market.

rationales.A: Incorrect. TRACE is a REPORTING system for OTC bond transactions, not a trading venue.
rationales.B:
Full statement: a separate ex-post liquidity gauge specific to the US corporate bond market — the Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine reporting system introduced in 2002 to track real-time price and volume reporting for bond transactions; portfolio managers review recent TRACE trading activity to gauge estimated cost of trading a bond position.

Correct. Per the reading, TRACE is 'a separate, ex-post liquidity gauge specific to the US corporate bond market... the TRACE (Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine) reporting system introduced in 2002 to track real-time price and volume reporting for bond transactions. Portfolio managers will often review recent TRACE trading activity to gauge the estimated cost of trading a bond position.' Complements the effective spread transaction cost statistic for actively quoted bonds.
rationales.C: Incorrect. It is the Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine — an ex-post transaction-reporting gauge, not an investment product.

options.A: Managers control liquidity risk by trading their most illiquid positions as frequently as possible.
options.B: Managers control liquidity risk by avoiding all fixed-income exposure whenever spreads widen.
options.C: favor on-the-run government bonds or most recently issued corporate/other bonds for short-term tactical positioning while reserving relatively illiquid positions for buy-and-hold strategies.

rationales.A: Incorrect. High-frequency trading of illiquid names maximizes transaction costs — the opposite of the practice.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Exposure avoidance is not the technique; using on-the-run and recent issues for tactical trades while reserving illiquid positions for buy-and-hold is.
rationales.C:
Full statement: favor on-the-run government bonds or most recently issued corporate/other bonds for short-term tactical positioning while reserving relatively illiquid positions for buy-and-hold strategies; consider liquid alternatives such as CDS and bond ETFs to close portfolio gaps or react quickly to changing markets.

Correct. Per the reading: 'First, active managers will usually favor on-the-run government bonds or most recently issued corporate or other bonds for short-term tactical portfolio positioning, while reserving relatively illiquid positions for buy-and-hold strategies or strategic positioning to minimize expected return erosion due to trading costs. Second, active managers might consider liquid alternatives to individual bond trades to close portfolio gaps where active management adds little value, or to react quickly to rapidly changing markets. These alternatives include CDS and bond ETFs.'

options.A: liquid, exchange-traded bond portfolios that create and redeem shares using an OTC primary market between institutional investors and the ETF sponsor.
options.B: Fixed-income ETFs are economically identical to holding the individual underlying bonds directly.
options.C: Fixed-income ETFs are actively managed products carrying high management fees.

rationales.A:
Full statement: liquid, exchange-traded bond portfolios that create and redeem shares using an OTC primary market between institutional investors and the ETF sponsor; ETF shares trade on an exchange, overcoming liquidity constraints of OTC-traded bonds; usually offer relatively constant portfolio duration (with exception of target-maturity ETFs).

Correct. Per the reading, 'Fixed-income ETFs are liquid, exchange-traded bond portfolios that create and redeem shares using an OTC primary market that exists between a set of institutional investors (or authorized participants) and the ETF sponsor. These ETF shares trade in the secondary market on an exchange, overcoming the liquidity constraints of individual OTC-traded bonds. Bond ETFs have enjoyed significant growth. As ETF sponsors target a specific index or profile, ETFs offer relatively constant portfolio duration and pay variable monthly interest based on the overall portfolio.' Active credit managers use ETFs to quickly overweight/underweight exposures in rapidly changing markets.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Exchange tradability, the creation/redemption mechanism, and unit economics distinguish ETFs from direct bond ownership.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Most bond ETFs are index-tracking with LOW fees; the creation/redemption OTC primary market underpins their liquidity.

options.A: Dealers quote narrower bid-ask spreads on illiquid bonds to compensate buyers for holding them.
options.B: Bid-ask spreads on illiquid bonds are identical to those on liquid bonds under competitive quoting.
options.C: wider because dealers must finance inventories, less liquid bonds remain in inventory longer, and dealers seek to cover funding/holding costs and profit through the spread.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The direction is opposite — illiquidity WIDENS quoted spreads.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Spreads vary systematically with liquidity: longer inventory residence and financing costs are passed into wider quotes.
rationales.C:
Full statement: wider because dealers must finance inventories, less liquid bonds remain in inventory longer, and dealers seek to cover funding/holding costs and profit through the spread; some illiquid bonds won't have quotes.

Correct. Per the reading, dealers cover their costs and make a profit through the bid-ask spread — the spread will be higher for illiquid bonds that are likely to remain in inventory longer. Bond dealers often carry inventory because buy/sell orders don't arrive simultaneously. Riskier bonds have higher bid-ask spreads due to dealers' aversion to hold them. Government bond spreads are lowest of all; conventional corporate spreads lower than complex/embedded-option corporates.

options.A: The protection buyer pays 2.375% upfront because the CDS spread exceeds the standard coupon.
options.B: receives 2.375% upfront (= (1.00% − 0.50%) × 4.75) because CDS spread is below the standard IG coupon of 1.00%.
options.C: The protection buyer receives 0.5% upfront, equal to the CDS spread itself.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The buyer RECEIVES the upfront — the 0.50% spread is BELOW the 1.00% standard IG coupon.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 22: 'Because investment-grade CDS contracts have a fixed coupon of 1.00% p.a. versus the 0.50% p.a. CDS market spread, the investor buying protection should receive the difference from par upfront in exchange for paying an above-market coupon under the contract.' Upfront premium: 2.375% of CDS notional = (1.00% − 0.50%) × 4.75. Standard fixed coupons post-2008 are 1.00% for IG and 5.00% for HY.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The upfront applies the spread-coupon gap times EffSpreadDur: (1.00% − 0.50%) × 4.75 = 2.375%.

options.A: Markit CDX North American Investment Grade index consisting of 125 equally weighted CDS contracts on entities (with six subindexes.
options.B: The most frequently traded US investment-grade CDS index is the European iTraxx Crossover index.
options.C: The most frequently traded US investment-grade CDS index is the S&P 500 index.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Markit CDX North American Investment Grade index consisting of 125 equally weighted CDS contracts on entities (with six subindexes: High Volatility, Consumer Cyclical, Energy, Financials, Industrial, Telecom Media & Technology).

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, the Markit CDX North American Investment Grade index consists of 125 equally weighted CDS contracts on entities, including six subindexes (High Volatility, Consumer Cyclical, Energy, Financials, Industrial, and Telecom, Media, and Technology).' The 5-year tenor is generally the most frequently traded contract.
rationales.B: Incorrect. iTraxx Crossover covers European sub-investment-grade names.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The S&P 500 is an equity index; the CDX North American Investment Grade (125 equally weighted names) is the instrument.

options.A: $292,250 (= $217,250 from price appreciation + $75,000 from incremental coupon income due to credit spreads).
options.B: None of the $506,500 rolldown gain is attributable to credit spreads; the entire amount reflects the government curve.
options.C: The full $506,500 gain is attributable to credit spread roll-down, with no benchmark contribution.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Example 26: The total incremental roll-down strategy generates $506,500 (= $344,000 price appreciation + $162,500 coupon income), of which $292,250 (= $217,250 + $75,000) is estimated to be due to credit spread curve roll down. The $75,000 is the credit-spread portion of incremental coupon ($225,000 − $150,000). The $217,250 is half the 0.07% YTM decline attributable to yield spread narrowing (from 0.85% to 0.815% at 14.5y).
rationales.B: Incorrect. The credit component is substantial — $292,250 of the total.
rationales.C: Incorrect. $506,500 is the TOTAL; the spread portion is $292,250 ($217,250 price appreciation plus $75,000 incremental spread-driven coupon).

options.A: Expecting HY spreads to widen versus IG, the manager buys HY bonds and sells IG bonds.
options.B: sells (goes short in) HY bonds and buys (goes long in) IG bonds, or synthetically: buys protection on HY CDS index and sells protection on IG CDS index (Example 29.
options.C: Expecting relative spread widening, the manager makes no portfolio changes.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Long HY loses as HY spreads widen — the position is backwards for the view.
rationales.B:
Full statement: sells (goes short in) HY bonds and buys (goes long in) IG bonds, or synthetically: buys protection on HY CDS index and sells protection on IG CDS index (Example 29 — CDX HY vs CDX IG).

Correct. Per the reading: 'an active manager expects a weaker economy and a widening of high-yield versus investment-grade credit spread levels. The manager can capitalize on this view by buying five-year protection on a high-yield CDS index and selling protection on an investment-grade CDS index for the same tenor.' Example 29 shows this: sell CDX IG protection, buy CDX HY protection; under an economic downturn where IG spreads rise 50% and HY spreads double, the strategy gains $697,000.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The view demands positioning: short HY/long IG in cash, or buy HY index protection and sell IG index protection synthetically.

options.A: Derivative-based strategies to add credit spread exposure consist only of buying CDS protection.
options.B: Managers seeking additional spread duration must avoid derivatives entirely and buy cash bonds.
options.C: selling CDS single-name or index protection for longer maturities or lower credit quality, or using a long-short approach.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Buying protection REDUCES credit exposure; selling protection adds it.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Selling single-name or index protection — longer maturity or lower quality — is the derivative route to added spread duration.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading, 'Derivative-based credit strategies to add credit spread duration or increase credit exposure include selling CDS single-name or index protection for longer maturities or lower credit quality or using a long-short approach to achieve a similar objective.' Selling protection creates 'long risk' exposure (analogous to owning bonds); buying protection creates 'short risk' exposure (analogous to shorting bonds).

options.A: The Bloomberg SRSK model estimates sovereign credit risk using political risk as its only input.
options.B: a combination of quantitative and qualitative factors — external debt to GDP, currency reserves, GDP growth, and political risk — to estimate a sovereign issuer's one-year POD.
options.C: The SRSK model relies only on historical default rates, extrapolating past sovereign defaults forward.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Political risk is one factor among several — external debt/GDP, currency reserves, and GDP growth join it.
rationales.B:
Full statement: a combination of quantitative and qualitative factors — external debt to GDP, currency reserves, GDP growth, and political risk — to estimate a sovereign issuer's one-year POD; similar to DRSK, users can change model inputs.

Correct. Per the reading, 'The Bloomberg Sovereign Risk (SRSK) model shown in Exhibit 30 combines quantitative and qualitative factors such as external debt to GDP, currency reserves, GDP growth, and political risk to estimate a sovereign issuer's one-year POD. Similar to the DRSK model discussed earlier, the SRSK model allows users to change model inputs and also derives a "model" CDS spread, which could be compared to the market CDS spread.' Costa Rica example: 1.28% one-year default risk.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The model is forward-looking, combining quantitative macro-financial variables with qualitative factors.

options.A: Emerging markets exhibit characteristics identical to developed markets across growth, risk, and market access.
options.B: higher, more volatile, and less balanced economic growth than developed markets, greater geopolitical risk, currency restrictions, and capital controls.
options.C: Emerging market investments carry no currency risk because local currencies are pegged to the dollar.

rationales.A: Incorrect. EM growth is higher, more volatile, and less balanced, with greater geopolitical and capital-flow risk.
rationales.B:
Full statement: higher, more volatile, and less balanced economic growth than developed markets, greater geopolitical risk, currency restrictions, and capital controls — sovereign credit risk is a critical starting point.

Correct. Per the reading, 'Emerging markets are characterized by higher, more volatile, and less balanced economic growth than developed markets, often in addition to greater geopolitical risk, currency restrictions, and capital controls. Sovereign credit risk is therefore a critical starting point in considering fixed-income investments in emerging markets, where both the ability and willingness of issuers to repay debt is of importance.' Institutional considerations include political stability, transparency, adherence to property rights and contract law.
rationales.C: Incorrect. EM currencies are often volatile, and currency restrictions and controls add to — rather than remove — FX risk.

options.A: the relationship between the higher local currency YTM, the lower USD bond YTM, and the local currency's expected depreciation vs USD over the investment horizon.
options.B: The choice between the local-currency and USD bond depends only on the yield-to-maturity differential.
options.C: The choice depends only on the issuer's credit risk, which differs between the two bonds.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the relationship between the higher local currency YTM, the lower USD bond YTM, and the local currency's expected depreciation vs USD over the investment horizon; if bullish on the emerging economy's growth prospects, the investor may earn higher USD return from the unhedged local currency bond if the local currency depreciates less than expected under interest rate parity conditions (forward rate bias).

Correct. Per Example 34 solution: 'A United States-based investor seeking to maximize US-dollar-denominated return must consider the relationship between the higher local currency bond YTM, the lower US dollar bond YTM, and the local currency's expected depreciation (or appreciation) versus the US dollar over the investment horizon. While uncovered interest rate parity suggests that local currency depreciation vs the US dollar would offset any benefit of a higher YTM, an investor with a bullish view of the emerging economy's growth prospects would benefit from forward rate bias and earn a higher return in US dollar terms from an unhedged investment in the local currency bond if the local currency were to depreciate less than expected.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The higher local yield can be consumed by currency depreciation — the FX view is essential.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The issuer is the SAME; the decision weighs the YTM gap against expected local-currency depreciation versus USD.

options.A: Structured products offer active credit managers no additional benefits over corporate bonds, since securitized cash flows carry the same risk-return profile as the issuing institutions' senior unsecured debt.
options.B: Structured products offer only lower returns than corporate bonds of comparable rating, because the costs of structuring and servicing consume any yield advantage the underlying collateral provides.
options.C: the ability to access fixed-income cash flows (commercial/residential real estate, consumer loans), enhance returns by increasing exposure to interest rate volatility (via mortgage prepayment/extension risk), and add debt exposure created by redistribution of default risk into tranches across the credit spectrum.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Access to distinct cash-flow pools (real estate, consumer loans) and exposure tailoring are genuine benefits.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Tranche selection and structural leverage can ENHANCE returns relative to comparable corporates.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading, 'Structured financial instruments can offer active credit managers the ability to access fixed-income cash flows such as commercial or residential real estate, enhance returns by increasing portfolio exposure to interest rate volatility (via mortgage prepayment and extension risk), and add debt exposure created by the redistribution of default risk into different tranches across the credit spectrum.'

options.A: establishes higher-rated tranches via internal credit enhancement or overcollateralization, with successively lower-rated tranches absorbing a greater proportion of the associated default risk.
options.B: Tranching gives all tranches in an ABS or CDO identical risk, differing only in coupon convention.
options.C: Tranching eliminates default risk entirely from the securitization structure.

rationales.A:
Full statement: establishes higher-rated tranches via internal credit enhancement or overcollateralization, with successively lower-rated tranches absorbing a greater proportion of the associated default risk — an SPV owns the underlying asset pool and issues debt across several tranches backed by the asset pool cash flows.

Correct. Per the reading and Exhibit 32: 'This is achieved by establishing higher-rated tranches via internal credit enhancement or overcollateralization, with successively lower-rated tranches absorbing a greater proportion of the associated default risk. An active investor might overweight default risk by choosing a lower-rated ABS tranche based on a tactical view. Alternatively, a portfolio manager might underweight credit exposure using a higher-rated tranche in a downturn.' The ABS issuer is a special purpose vehicle (SPV) that owns the underlying asset pool.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Stratification is the point — senior tranches are protected by subordination while lower tranches absorb losses first.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Default risk is REDISTRIBUTED across the capital structure, not eliminated.

options.A: Covered bonds differ from ABS in offering no recourse to the issuing institution.
options.B: covered bonds offer dual recourse (i.e., to both the issuing financial institution and the underlying asset pool) as well as substitution of non-performing assets.
options.C: Covered bonds differ from ABS in always carrying the highest yield among secured instruments.

rationales.A: Incorrect. DUAL recourse — to issuer AND asset pool — is the covered bond's defining feature.
rationales.B:
